Aformer fracking site North Yorkshire village Kirby Misperton, once lightning rod environmental protests, may soon new frontier Britain' clean energy revolution. first time UK, abandoned gas well begin second life source geothermal energy.
far cry beginnings highly contested site where frackers hoped tap fresh reservoirs gas trapped layers shale beneath earth' surface. 2016, Third Energy granted permission carry out fracking existing well plans ultimately thwarted government moratorium using technology UK.
transformation being led CeraPhi Energy, made team former oil gas veterans who plan use expertise drilling fossil fuels harness potential renewable energy lying dormant beneath earth' surface.
After 30 years fossil fuel sector, Karl Farrow founded company 2020, midst Covid-19 pandemic. plan, said, bring together people deep expertise oil gas industry unlock new source clean energy.
" first thing did when got keys gates invite leaders local anti-fracking groups around see what planning site," said. "I think many them see project kind closure. doing something positive here. So all work trying prevent fracking now likely lead clean energy result."
company "natural transition" reuse existing drilling expertise infrastructure tap renewable energy below. Drilling fossil fuels drilling geothermal "virtually identical", Farrow said. " only difference you're effectively extracting renewable energy source rather fossil fuel."
Typically, harnessing geothermal energy involves drilling borehole depths two three miles, flow cold water low pressures through hot rocks beneath Earth' surface. Then, second borehole returns warm water surface where can used heat homes businesses or generate electricity.
Unlike fracking there very little environmental risk. well sealed water lines operate closed loop system meaning there no exposure subsurface. "Think pushing test tube into some sand. creates completely sealed environment," Farrow said.
Kirby Misperton project final testing phase: so far results proven Farrow' theories engineering calculations. some cases says they better expected. His team had anticipated temperatures 90 degrees Celsius base wells, instead recorded temperatures 110 degrees. Farrow hopes share findings community, local authorities government bodies make case Britain' geothermal potential.
" next few months hopefully start developing site wells into real projects," said. Ultimately, Farrow expects heat captured geothermal wells used local heat networks used warm blocks flats, schools, hospitals, office parks or leisure centres.
"I think it' only direct competitor respect gas heating," Farrow said. "I think honestly make 15-20% energy mix heat going forward, if." date, major oil companies treated geothermal " bit science project" Farrow believes world now moving towards "geothermal decade".
CeraPhi nine potential geothermal projects UK, Farrow says burgeoning industry still needs push government accelerate pace can play role decarbonising heating.
UK' early steps into geothermal energy include project Seaham, County Durham, use water mineshafts heat 1,500 new homes, scheme Eden Project Cornwall, generates heat warm Eden' indoor rainforest Mediterranean biomes well offices, kitchens greenhouses.
Geothermal energy offers potential source clean heating - serendipitous economic boost many areas government found greatest need new investment part levelling plan.
Research University Durham found these include Redcar Cleveland, Middlesbrough, East Lindsey, Hartlepool, Northumberland Bassetlaw, all appear top 10 index used government identify local authority areas need levelling. Other areas well-suited producing geothermal heat electricity include Newcastle upon Tyne, North East Derbyshire, East Riding Yorkshire Nottingham.
theory, UK enough geothermal energy trapped underground heat every home hundred years. Even conservative estimates account realistic commercial logistical limits, geothermal energy help UK cut imports fossil fuels rely only North Sea gas.
"Geothermal still very low profile," said. " needs come out shadows into light."

Britain' only remaining coal power plant Ratcliffe--Soar Nottinghamshire generate electricity last time Monday after powering UK 57 years.
power plant come end life line government' world-leading policy phase out coal power first signalled almost decade ago.
closure marks end Britain' 142- history coal power use began when world' first coal-fired power station, Holborn Viaduct power station, began generating electricity 1882.
shutdown hailed green campaigners major achievement government reducing UK' carbon emissions, providing international climate leadership, ensuring "just transition" staff Britain' coal industry.
Michael Shanks, minister energy, said: "Today' closure Ratcliffe marks end era coal workers can rightly proud work powering our country 140 years. owe generations debt gratitude country."
UK became first country set end date coal power 2025 after putting place increasingly stringent green regulations reduce running hours coal plants.
Ministers strengthened UK' leadership phasing out coal calling deadline come forward, shortly before UK hosted UN' Cop26 climate talks Glasgow late 2021.
Ratcliffe' 170 remaining staff invited gather canteen Monday where live stream power plant' control room show moment generating units turned off last time.
Peter O'Grady, Ratcliffe' plant manager, said: " whole series poignant moments. I'm sure there few tears whole thing stops people leave."
coal plant once employed 3,000 engineers workforce declined line power output recent years. Coal power made 80% UK' electricity early 1980s, 40% 2012, before petering out last decade due costly carbon taxes rise cheaper renewables.
" final chapter remarkably swift transition country started industrial revolution," said Phil MacDonald, managing director global energy thinktank Ember.
report Ember found coal power halved among Organisation Economic Co-operation Development (OECD) countries since reaching peak 2007. Coal power made 17% electricity generated OECD countries last, according Ember, 27 38 member states pledged coal-free end decade.
Ed Matthew, director climate crisis thinktank E3G, said: " UK first country build coal-fired power station. right first major economy exit coal power. true global leadership, lighting path other countries follow."
Tony Bosworth, campaigner Friends Earth, said: " priority now move away gas well, developing fast possible UK' huge homegrown renewable energy potential delivering economic boost bring. vital green transition must fair, protecting workers benefiting communities."
Staff first told 2021 plant would close late 2022 Ratcliffe' owner, German energy company Uniper, later said would keep plant running during Europe-wide gas crisis triggered Russia' invasion Ukraine under agreement government.
Uniper worked unions help many engineers into new jobs company' other power plants or into training lead work other areas energy industry. 100 expected remain plant carry out decommissioning work next two years.
Michael Lewis, Uniper' chief executive, said: " me, Ratcliffe always just power station - pillar UK' energy security decades. Built during time when coal backbone industrial progress, Ratcliffe powered 2m homes businesses - equivalent entire East Midlands region. played crucial role boosting economic growth supporting livelihoods thousands people.
" first time since 1882 coal powered Great Britain. close chapter, honour Ratcliffe' legacy people working here, while embracing future cleaner flexible energy," said.
article amended 30 September 2024 include Drax power station map, remove graph showing different types fuel used electricity generation UK 1920 onwards.

Fewer one three voters believe Australia track meet Albanese government' target net zero emissions 2050, according Guardian Essential poll.
latest poll 1,149 voters found half Australians support nuclear energy. Although renewables remain popular, local impact transmission infrastructure concern.
findings help explain Peter Dutton' political strategy talk potential nuclear power criticise transition renewable energy unfeasible or harmful local communities.
opposition leader Coalition yet provide any detail how they would support nuclear industry, beyond removing Australia' prohibition nuclear power experts agree power source would take decades get running.
Guardian Essential poll, released Tuesday, found 55% respondents agree human pollution activity "main reasons global warming", compared just 22% who disagreed.
poll found 38% voters say Australia doing enough combat climate change, lowest proportion seven years coming off highs 62% who said same January 2020 height black summer bushfires.
Some 36% said Australia doing enough combat climate change, while 17% said doing too much. further 9% said they didn't know.
Respondents sceptical reaching net zero 2050: 7% said "very likely" 24% agreed "quite likely" - combined total only 31%. compared 38% who said " likely" 19% who responded " likely all". further 11% unsure.
Majorities said Australia doing enough preserve endangered species (53%), oceans rivers (52%), native forests (52%). They pleased pace transition renewables, 45% saying Australia doing enough 40% calling action.
Support most renewable infrastructure high, majorities backing solar farms (69%), offshore wind (60%), onshore wind (59%) community batteries (56%). overground transmission supported just 35% respondents.
Presented series propositions transition, 70% said development renewables should "come expense local communities".
Two-thirds (66%) said Australia needs make most resources weather conditions address climate change. Three-fifths (60%) said Australia needs rapidly develop renewables because they cheaper create new jobs.
Just quarter (26%) said Australia does need transition renewables.
Asked Australia building nuclear power plants generate electricity, 23% strongly favour, further 27% somewhat favour.
same proportion (50%) backed nuclear September 2021, although proportion those strongly favour increased three points since then. One three voters (33%) oppose nuclear power today.
Renewables appear bad rap when comes price, 38% respondents ranking them "most expensive" three options, followed 34% who ranked nuclear most expensive 28% who said fossil fuels.
climate change energy minister, Chris Bowen, repeatedly rebuffed Dutton' nuclear push, citing cost - including estimate energy department replacing fossil fuels nuclear cost $387bn.
Earlier October Bowen accused Coalition using " rightwing playbook 2023 - populism, polarisation post-truth politics" making false claims potential nuclear power Australia.

Asked what most important drivers power prices, respondents blamed: excessive profits energy companies (28%), efforts fight climate change including shift renewable energy (19%), ageing electricity network (16%), international circumstances such war Ukraine (13%) restrictions oil gas (11%). further 13% unsure.
Essential poll found 50% voters had much, hardly any or no trust government lead energy transition, compared 42% who said they had lot or fair amount.
separate Australian National University poll, released Tuesday, found 77.4% Australians satisfied or very satisfied democracy, compared 81% 2008.
poll, released ahead ANU' Crawford leadership forum, recorded big decrease Australians being "very satisfied" democracy, down 23.4% 15 years ago 14.2% today.

Australia' big private electricity generators dismissed nuclear energy viable source power customers least decade.
They say they remain focused developing renewable sources coal gas plants exit grid.
comments - AGL Energy, Alinta, EnergyAustralia Origin Energy - follow announcement opposition leader, Peter Dutton, Coalition would back both large-scale small modular nuclear reactors (SMR) way cut electricity prices increase grid reliability.
Energy Australia, whose Hong Kong-listed owner CLP currently operates two large nuclear power stations mainland China, said company "committed Australia' clean energy transformation, reducing emissions quickly affordably possible while maintaining system reliability".
" know Australia need some form controllable long-duration, zero-carbon storage or generation deliver net zero 2050," EA spokesperson said, adding green hydrogen or nuclear had potential play role.
"Given long lead times development, [nuclear ] potential option late 2030s or 2040s."
Alinta said had approached Coalition nuclear energy " something exploring".
" viable option, regulatory environment would need amended many other considerations would need assessed," Alinta spokesperson said.
Damien Nicks, AGL' chief, said nuclear energy part company' plans develop coal gas plants into low-emissions industrial hubs.
"There no viable schedule regulation or development nuclear energy Australia cost, build time public opinion all prohibitive," Nicks said Friday. "Policy certainty important companies like AGL ongoing debate matter runs risk unnecessarily complicating long-term investment decisions necessary energy transition."
Origin spokesperson said company focused accelerating take- renewables storage, including construction big batteries Eraring coal-fired power plant New South Wales Mortlake gas-fired plant Victoria.
" stage, our primary focus adding supply these mature low-emissions technologies, however continue watch progress any emerging technologies may able contribute emissions reduction time," spokesperson said.
Australia' electricity prices jumped recent years, including hike default market offer 2022 Morrison government delayed until after election - lumping Labor increase.
slow rollout wind solar farms, however, stoked concerns electricity sector won't sufficient capacity meet demand ageing coal plants shut.
While companies stress they remain "energy agnostic", challenges introducing new energy source requiring complex regulations, particularly storage disposal nuclear energy waste, steep, they say. They point absence commercially proven SMRs cost blowouts large-scale plants such UK' Hinkley Point C, touted world' "most expensive" power station.
Guardian Australia sought comment Ted O'Brien, opposition' energy spokesperson. "If you're serious nuclear, you're serious net zero," O'Brien said last December. " open all technologies renewables carbon, capture storage, zero-emissions nuclear energy, so much."
One senior executive told Guardian Australia power bills would triple if nuclear path pursued.
NSW' chief scientist, Hugh Durrant-Whyte, dismissed comparisons nuclear energy advocates places such Ontario, Canada. country had spent decades building nuclear industry employing 70,000 people.
"Nobody country even faintest idea how build nuclear power plant," Durrant-Whyte, former nuclear adviser UK government, told NSW upper house estimates earlier month.

G20 poured record levels public money into fossil fuels last despite having promised reduce some, report found.
amount public money flowing into coal, oil gas 20 world' biggest economies reached record $1.4tn(£1.1tn) 2022, according International Institute Sustainable Development (IISD) thinktank, even though world leaders agreed phase out "inefficient" fossil fuel subsidies Cop26 climate summit Glasgow two years ago.
report comes ahead meeting G20 countries Delhi next month set tone next big climate conference, takes place United Arab Emirates November.
crucial leaders put fossil fuel subsidies agenda, said Tara Laan, senior associate IISD lead author study. "These figures stark reminder massive amounts public money G20 governments continue pour into fossil fuels - despite increasingly devastating impacts climate change."
Fossil fuels release pollutants when burned heat planet make extreme weather violent. They dirty air toxins damage people' lungs other organs. Scientists estimate air pollution fossil fuels kills between 1 10 million people each.
beyond overlooked costs society, governments lowered prices further supporting fossil fuel producers customers public money. report found G20 governments last provided fossil fuels $1tn subsidies, $322bn investments state-owned enterprises $50bn loans public finance institutions.
total amount double what they had provided 2019, authors found.
G20 leaders agreed phase out inefficient fossil fuel subsidies " medium term" far back 2009. Cop26 climate summit decade later, world leaders agreed accelerate these efforts.
since then, living costs risen sharply result Covid-19 pandemic Russian invasion Ukraine. latter sparked energy crisis prompted many governments intervene costs fuel cap energy bills.
Scientists doctors long warned dangers subsidising fuels kill people while blocking efforts clean economies. recently, energy experts economists joined chorus voices calling fossil fuel subsidies into question.
February, report International Energy Agency found scale fossil fuel subsidies 2022 "worrying sign energy transitions", though argued some measures defended social or political necessities "given hardship full exposure market-driven prices caused".
June, report World Bank found " underpricing fossil fuels, governments only incentivise overuse, perpetuate inefficient polluting technologies entrench inequality".
authors found all subsidies energy sector, three-quarters go fossil fuels.
"There huge potential subsidy reform," said Richard Damania, chief economist sustainability group World Bank lead author study. " repurposing wasteful subsidies, can free significant sums instead used address some planet' most pressing challenges."
added: "Governments should prioritise reforms build public acceptance, protect most vulnerable, show how money being spent meaningfully improve people' lives."
IISD found setting higher carbon tax $25-75 per ton greenhouse gases, G20 governments raise extra $1tn.
offset costs vulnerable people living poverty, authors recommended targeted welfare payments those who need most. They recommended world leaders drop qualifier "inefficient" promise phase out fossil fuel subsidies, instead name exceptional cases where they would justified.
IISD called G20 leaders end fossil fuel subsidies rich countries 2025 rest 2030.
" fossil fuel companies gaining record profits amid energy crisis last, there little incentive them change business models line what' needed limit global warming," said Laan. " governments power push them right direction."

Clean concrete, steel, aluminum pasta
Industrial processes contribute quarter all planet-heating emissions US sector considered difficult green, unlike other areas, such electrification cars or adding solar wind power grid.
High-heat activities such creating steel or concrete still reliant fossil fuels Biden administration said can reversed via $6bn new spending cutting-edge technologies. Jennifer Granholm, US energy secretary, said 33 funded projects represent " single largest industrial decarbonization investment American history".
projects include new aluminum casting plant West Virginia run hydrogen, rather gas - first kind. Six different projects create zero emission or low-emissions cement, key ingredient concrete, Kraft Heinz, food manufacturer, get $170.9m install electric boilers heat pumps facilities create, among other things, pasta without fossil fuels. #72
Britain' transition low-carbon future reached milestone closure last remaining coal-fired power plant Ratcliffe--Soar Nottinghamshire.
shutdown 57--old power plant Monday ends 140 years coal power generation UK - industrial story closely interwoven Britain' socioeconomic political history.
Between point when Britain' first coal power plant began generating 1882 until Ratcliffe' closure, UK' coal plants burned through 4.6bn tonnes coal emitted 10.4bn tonnes carbon dioxide, according analysts Carbon Brief, most countries ever produced all fossil fuel sources.
end era welcomed trade unions, green campaigners politicians exemplar how polluting industries can wound down while safeguarding labour force communities. provides chance look back reflect central role coal played powering modern Britain:
coal revolution
Britain' reliance coal dates back Roman empire. British Geological Survey believes Romans may begun mining Nettlebridge area Somerset, England, close Fosse Way, source coal used fuel heat baths forge iron. Industrial Revolution when coal came into own crucial driver Britain' emerging industrial might.
Demand coal exploded 1700s thanks population boom. Initially, nascent British mining industry struggled keep demand, invention steam engine 1712 unlocked potential Britain' abundant reserves coalfields central Scotland, south Wales, Midlands north-east England.
Coal-driven Newcomen engines pumped out water regularly flooded mines, affording access deeper plentiful reserves. early 1700s, 3m tonnes coal mined every - number exploded 30m tonnes 1830s.
early 1800s, coal used make town gas lighting fuel expansion Britain' burgeoning railways. only 1882 Britain opened world' first public coal-fired power station London' Holborn Viaduct. plant, built Thomas Edison' Edison Electric Light Company, initially generated enough electricity almost 1,000 street lights Holborn Circus St Martin' Le Grand well private residences. Other small-scale coal power plants followed.
fossil-fuel century
start 20th century almost 100% Britain' electricity generated coal plants. 1950, dominance remained largely unchallenged 96%, late 1960s 1970s Britain' state-owned Central Electricity Generating Board ushered new generation super coal plants.
1966, Ferrybridge C began generating electricity River Aire West Yorkshire. opened generating capacity 2,000 megawatts four 500MW units, first Europe create electricity generating unit size.
Within two years, coal plants similar size mushroomed across Britain' coalfields: Ratcliffe--Soar, Cottam West Burton all began generating power Nottinghamshire; Eggborough power plant started operations North Yorkshire.
end decade, Ironbridge Rugeley coal power plants began generating power West Midlands. total, 12 power stations scale began generating electricity years between 1966 1974, culminating giant Drax plant North Yorkshire.
After sharp slump during 1984-1985 miners' strike coal power failed return highs recorded start decade. 1990s, Britain' dash gas power rising environmental concerns spelled beginning long existential decline coal.
Powering past coal
millennium began coal accounting 36% UK' electricity; newer gas power plants nuclear reactors playing larger role power system. Coal plants became increasingly expensive run owing regulations called costly upgrades help reduce pollution.
2008, UK government passed Climate Change Act - mandating 80% cut overall six greenhouse gases including carbon dioxide emissions 2050 - raising questions future coal generation, produces double carbon emissions newer gas power plants.
2013, new tax designed top cost carbon emissions tipped scales further favour gas. tax largely responsible shutdown 10 UK' largest coal power plants next decade.
2015, government set out plans end all coal-fired power generation entirely 2025. same, global governments met Paris UN climate talks, leading 2016 Paris climate agreement keep rise global surface temperature well below 2C.
2020, coal power had dwindled just 1.8% UK' electricity mix, 2021 government brought forward ban October 2024. decision hasten end Britain' coal power era announced just months before UK hosted Cop26 UN climate talks Glasgow, where calls phase out coal took centre stage.
Ratcliffe plant had originally planned shut late 2022 owner, German energy company Uniper, later said would keep plant running during Europe-wide gas crisis triggered Russia' invasion Ukraine under agreement government.
Michael Shanks, minister energy, said closure marked end era, adding: "Coal workers can rightly proud work powering our country 140 years. owe generations debt gratitude country."
#73
Coal-fired power plants would forced capture smokestack emissions or shut down under rule issued Thursday Environmental Protection Agency (EPA).
New limits greenhouse gas emissions fossil fuel-fired electric plants Biden administration' most ambitious effort yet roll back planet-warming pollution power sector, nation' second-largest contributor climate crisis. rules key part Joe Biden' pledge eliminate carbon pollution electricity sector 2035 economy-wide 2050.
rule among four separate measures targeting coal natural gas plants EPA said would provide "regular certainty" power industry encourage them make investments transition " clean energy economy". They include requirements reduce toxic wastewater pollutants coal-fired plants safely manage so-called coal ash unlined storage ponds.
new rules "reduce pollution fossil fuel-fired power plants, protect communities pollution improve public health - all while supporting long-term, reliable supply electricity needed power America forward", EPA administrator, Michael Regan, told reporters White House briefing.
plan likely challenged industry groups Republican-leaning states. They repeatedly accused Democratic administration overreach environmental regulations warned looming reliability crisis electric grid. rules issued Thursday among least half-dozen EPA regulations limiting power plant emissions wastewater pollution.
Environmental groups hailed EPA' latest action urgently needed protect against devastating harms climate crisis.
power plant rule marks first time federal government restricted carbon dioxide emissions existing coal-fired power plants. rule would force future electric plants fueled coal or gas control 90% carbon pollution. new standards stave off 1.38bn metric tons carbon pollution through 2047, equivalent annual emissions 328m gas cars, EPA said, provide hundreds billions dollars climate health benefits, measured fewer premature deaths, asthma cases, lost work or school days.
Coal plants plan stay open beyond 2039 would cut or capture 90% carbon dioxide emissions 2032, EPA said. Plants expect retire 2039 would face less stringent standard still would capture some emissions. Coal plants set retire 2032 would subject new rules.
Rich Nolan, president CEO National Mining Association, said through latest rules, " EPA systematically dismantling reliability US electric grid".
accused Biden, Regan other officials "ignoring our energy reality forcing closure well-operating coal plants repeatedly come rescue during times peak demand. repercussions reckless plan felt across country all Americans."
Regan denied rules aimed shutting down coal sector, acknowledged proposing power plant rule last " see some coal retirements".
proposal relies technologies limit carbon pollution industry itself said viable available, Regan said. "Multiple power companies indicated [carbon capture storage] viable technology power sector today, they currently pursuing those CCS projects," told reporters Wednesday.
Coal provided 16% US electricity last, down 45% 2010. Natural gas provides 43% US electricity, remainder nuclear energy renewables such wind, solar hydropower.
power plant rule "completes historic grand slam" major actions Biden administration reduce carbon pollution, said David Doniger, climate clean energy expert Natural Resources Defense Council. first most important action passage 2022 climate law, officially known Inflation Reduction Act, said, followed separate EPA rules targeting tailpipe emissions cars trucks methane emissions oil gas drilling.
#74
Fossil fuel lobbyists coordinated lawmakers behind scenes across state lines push shape laws escalating crackdown peaceful protests against oil gas expansion, new Guardian investigation reveals.
Records obtained Guardian show lobbyists working major North American oil gas companies key architects anti-protest laws increase penalties lead non-violent environmental climate activists being imprisoned 10 years.
Emails between fossil fuel lobbyists lawmakers Utah, West Virginia, Idaho Ohio suggest nationwide strategy deter people frustrated government failure tackle climate crisis peacefully disrupting expansion fossil fuel infrastructure enacting tough laws lengthy jail sentences.
"Draft bill attached," wrote lobbyist representing two influential fossil fuel trade groups lead counsel West Virginia state energy committee January 2020.
law, carries maximum 10- prison sentence, later used charge least eight peaceful climate protesters including six senior citizens.
Amid ongoing record oil gas expansion US, activists say they turned protests non-violent civil disobedience such blocking roads chaining themselves trees, machinery equipment way slow down construction, raise public awareness, press urgent climate action governments corporations.
Police arrest environmental activists protesting Line 3 oil sands pipeline near Itasca state park Minnesota 7 June 2021. Photograph: Kerem Yücel/AFP/Getty Images
Civil disobedience form political protest involves breaking law planned, symbolic way - activists rights experts say part bedrock democratic society tradition civil rights movements.
months-long investigation Guardian found companies lawmakers sought increase threat criminal action against activists protect oil gas expansion - even deadly destructive extreme weather events hit communities nationwide.
Last hottest record, wildfires, baking temperatures, deadly floods rising sea levels struck communities across North America - rest world. Under Biden administration, US handed out 1,450 new oil gas licences, accounting half total globally, 20% licences those issued Donald Trump, who promised "drill, baby, drill" should return White House.
findings dozens freedom information requests suggest right peaceful protest under attack US - much like other major democracies including UK, Germany, Canada Australia.
These countries, most responsible greenhouse gas emissions, continue back fossil fuel expansion fueling climate breakdown while cracking down activists groups sounding alarm - trend condemned Mary Lawlor, UN special rapporteur human rights defenders, "unacceptable".
"People taking peaceful action draw attention global warming, calling governments finally do something, human rights defenders - who US government states supports. [] must mean support all human rights defenders, even where they challenge action US state itself or interests powerful companies," said Lawlor.
"Existing legislation being misused or new legislation being brought criminalise peaceful acts calling real action combat climate change. unacceptable."
Utah, major oil, coal gas producer Rocky Mountain region, lawmakers passed anti-protest law carrying five years' jail time after discussing need protect fossil gas. " being forced out coal, cheap, reliable plentiful, nowhere go find replacement energy source because natural gas under attack," lawmaker wrote email January 2023, obtained Guardian.
Prosecuting non-violent climate protesters "just legalised violence", according Bill McKibben, environmentalist grassroots organiser who arrested participating peaceful climate protests.
"It' disgusting, it' deeply un-American, end won't stop transition cleaner world, do great damage good people organizations next few years."

volley anti-protest laws punishing civil disobedience felonies, fines long jail sentences passed states across US - response, least part, 2016 Indigenous-led non-violent uprising against Dakota Access oil pipeline Standing Rock Indian reservation.
Oklahoma, sixth-largest oil- gas-producing state US, first pass laws designed hold individuals "conspiring" organisations criminally civilly liable tampering or trespassing so-called critical infrastructure such pipelines. inspired nationwide push American Legislative Exchange Council (Alec), rightwing fossil fuel-funded group brings together corporations lawmakers behind closed doors craft model legislation environmental standards, reproductive rights voting, among other issues.
According International Center --Profit Law, 45 states considered new anti-protest legislation since 2017, 22 critical infrastructure bills enacted states including Wisconsin, North Dakota, Missouri, Arkansas, Florida Louisiana. critical infrastructure law passed Georgia 2023 carries penalty 20 years prison intentional damage critical infrastructure intention disrupting service. Louisiana, unauthorized entry around pipelines other oil gas facilities punishable imprisonment - or without hard labor five years.
So far, critical infrastructure laws led scores criminal civil charges against climate environmental activists several states.
includes three activists journalist Louisiana protesting against Dakota Access pipeline; 31 activists charged Texas after rappelling off bridge hang banners protesting against oil gas; eight protesters West Virginia criminally charged peacefully disrupting construction Mountain Valley pipeline (MVP), fossil fuel project forced through Democratic lawmaker Joe Manchin help supreme court.
Critics say wave critical infrastructure bills unnecessary states can use existing laws prosecute property destruction violence, these laws impinge freedom assembly, petition speech - first amendment rights.
Rico Sisney, who charged 2019 under Texas' critical infrastructure law, said felony charge violated his constitutional rights, leading him approach future protest activities " great deal caution".
"One things makes critical infrastructure laws so harmful they're essentially taking something would trespassing charge turning into felony," Sisney said. "It' essentially way fossil fuel industry lobbying make harder harder free speech or engage these kinds demonstrations."
Emails obtained through dozens freedom information requests Guardian found groups first initiated legislation 2017 - including Alec, Marathon Petroleum, American Fuel Petrochemical Manufacturers - continued pushing anti-protest laws different states, including most recently Idaho 2023.
Guardian found energy companies Dominion Rocky Mountain Power pushed anti-protest laws across different states.

It' scary, because direct assault our first amendment rights... find novel ways try criminalise [protest] just really disgusting disturbing shows moving towards fascism," said David Armiak, research director Center Media Democracy.
statement, Alec defended tactics. "Alec long supported free speech peaceful protests. What don't support, however, any criminal activity such trespassing or damaging critical infrastructure. Any suggestions contrary false," spokesperson Lars Dalseide said.
Dalseide claimed model legislation "does apply climate activists". "What does apply anyone who breaks into critical infrastructure facility /or causes damage facility, materials, or property. If those actions - I believe already against law before 2017 - required take part non-violent protest, then perhaps should re-examine definition non-violent," said.

midst fracking boom, helped propel position sixth-largest gas-producing state, Ohio became one first introduce Alec' model legislation 2018.

West Virginia: controversial pipeline
One biggest priorities recent years climate environmental activists Mountain Valley pipeline (MVP), 300-mile (480km) fossil gas pipeline stretching across Appalachian mountains Virginia West Virginia. 2018, construction crews began clearing dense forestland, protesters occupied trees along pipeline route. pipeline forecast add nearly 90m metric tons planet-heating emissions annually, equivalent 23 coal plants.
Fossil fuel lobbyists lawmakers already had close working relationship, according emails obtained Guardian, didn't take long gas industry respond protests suggesting critical infrastructure bill.
June 2019, Dominion lobbyist Robert Orndorff emailed Robert Akers, chief counsel West Virginia legislature' joint energy manufacturing committee, explain Amy Summers, house delegate, had reached out ask if they had "ever considered bill address civil disobedience towards pipelines", according email first published Intercept. (Dominion did respond Guardian' request comment. company told Intercept did initiate bill.)
"I know two years ago did pass bill created criminal offence trespass. I am sure addressed civil disobedience. Maybe Energy committee should consider such law," added Orndorff, copying other lobbyists into email including Greg Hoyer, who lobbied EQT Corporation, majority shareholder operator MVP.
November 2019, Hoyer had dinner chairman state energy committee Akers, committee' chief counsel. After dinner, forwarded Akers op-ed EQT' president CEO, Toby Rice, saying " industry needs spread good news, gas, abroad", according email obtained Guardian.
O'Neill registered lobbyist two powerful trade groups: American Fuel & Petrochemical Manufacturers (AFPM) Oil & Natural Gas Association West Virginia. (Neither trade group nor Akers responded requests comment.)
Just 10 days after email, 30 January 2020, lawmaker John Kelly introduced critical infrastructure protection bill, later admitted behest fossil fuel industry.
"Simple explanation, bill reinforces property rights. When person goes private property does intentional damage," Kelly wrote constituent who questioned legislation, email February 2020 first reported Intercept. " bill requested natural gas industry, because protesters entered drilling site destroyed equipment."
law, carried penalties one five years prison, passed spring 2020.
email, Chris Hall Orion Strategies took credit organising support law said "intended preempt acts intentional trespass damage critical infrastructure facilities".
"Orion Strategies would like thank all those involved supporting passage new law help ensure development some region' most important infrastructure projects," Hall wrote.
email response Guardian, Hall, Orion' chief financial administrative officer, said: "Orion Strategies firmly stands work essential bipartisan legislation increased penalties already-illegal activities terrorists criminals who destroying equipment endangering lives health workers private property job sites. law protects all critical infrastructure - including renewable energy, water sewer - just natural gas facilities."
2024, lawmakers amended law broaden offences punish some second-offence felonies 10 years prison.
" completion MVP beginning opportunity creation, our communities, America, our allies planet," said Rice, EQT' president, June, joint press conference Joe Manchin celebrate gas flowing through pipeline.
Utah: 'corporate capture' government

February 2023, three months after Alec' suggestion, Utah introduced critical infrastructure law punished peaceful protests against fossil fuel infrastructure five years jail.
Lawmaker Carl Albrecht, Alec member who long worked protect state' fossil fuel industry, publicly claimed law response attacks substations other states. emails obtained Guardian show Republican lawmakers brainstorming ways protect gas, they believed "under attack".
time, Albrecht working closely another Republican lawmaker, Colin Jack, "Alec policy champion", protect coal gas Utah. emails show Jack Albrecht asked several Utah power companies if they should pass law defining gas "renewable" so would " under less attack".
2023, days after Ohio' governor signed law defining gas "green energy", Jack wrote lobbyists several power utility companies, Albrecht, possibility passing similar legislation.
" being forced out coal, cheap, reliable, plentiful, nowhere go find replacement energy source because natural gas under attack. I wonder if there' any value us industry add natural gas code 'renewable' or something, so under less attack."
end, Albrecht introduced critical infrastructure bill, emails show made several amendments draft legislation per industry requests. one example, attorney major Utah power cooperative drafted language bill - specifically definition "critical infrastructure facility", included oil gas facilities.

one committee hearing, Raybould repeated arguments made Alec after substation attacks. another, she shared map committee showed other states critical infrastructure laws. committee member pointed out there already trespass law books state. Raybould replied her bill mirrored trespass language added "enhanced penalty". Another committee member asked if bill model legislation. Raybould answered: "No, however there other states who pursued similar legislation."
Records obtained Guardian show Raybould emailed various groups asking them testify support her bill, including lobbyist registered Rocky Mountain Power (RMP). Several companies including RMP replied say they supported bill. Raybould told committee Marathon Petroleum RMP supported legislation.

#75
US gas utilities partnering one nation' most trusted non-profits part "cynical PR stunt" combat efforts curb fossil fuel usage, Guardian investigation found.
Local Habitat Humanity affiliates teamed least four utilities across 10 states build "zero-net energy homes", meant produce energy they use.
houses, sold affordable rates low-income families, weatherized meticulously insulated boost efficiency equipped rooftop solar panels. they come appliances powered gas, planet-heating fossil fuel shown degrade indoor air quality.
utilities say homes provide lower electricity bills affordable mortgages vulnerable families.
"It' part our culture really give back communities serve," Jennifer Altieri, vice-president public affairs Atmos Energy' Colorado-Kansas division, said video announcing utility' first Habitat home Evans, Colorado, September 2021, provided single mother her children.
Yet Atmos privately described project something closer public relations tactic, intentionally launched state " frontlines" attempts quell reliance gas.
Atmos since expanded Habitat partnerships seven communities, plans launch similar initiatives least two. National Fuel north-east, SoCal Gas southern California, Nicor Illinois partnered Habitat zero-net energy (ZNE) projects.
moves utilities attach themselves social justice causes come US embroiled broader political culture war federal, state local attempts phase out gas make new buildings electric.
projects Atmos others won widespread praise gas interest groups. autumn 2022 feature Southeast Gas' magazine Natural Living says Atmos-Habitat partnerships promote "healthy living", same, Evans, Colorado, home finalist ESG award Southern Gas Association.

said Habitat' advocacy partnerships "designed confront housing crisis ensure people decent place call home". critics say those arrangements appear opened door gas utilities attach themselves organization reputation.

Zero-net energy homes
"Zero-net energy" building standard requiring home produce much energy uses. Builders first work boost home' efficiency insulation air sealing, then equip home energy production capacity, such solar panels.
efficient homes still use gas should considered climate-friendly, said Mike Henchen, who leads carbon-free buildings program pro-electrification non-profit RMI. "When comes climate, gas part problem," said. "There' effort hype natural gas clean energy, it'."
Gas primarily composed methane, greenhouse gas 80 times planet-warming carbon dioxide short term.
US, gas accounts vast majority planet-heating pollution buildings, makes one-10th all US greenhouse gas emissions.
utilities say projects illustrate gas can part decarbonization efforts. Describing Habitat partnerships American Gas Magazine, representative gas utility Nicor said: "These homes demonstrate importance natural gas part diverse energy mix net-zero future."
Jennifer Golz, spokesperson Nicor, said "research shows hybrid energy approach help drive greater emissions reductions lower overall costs" while improving reliability during extreme weather. spokesperson Atmos said: " believe balanced energy approach includes natural gas preserves energy choice, rather supporting specific fuels or technologies, achieve goals reducing emissions while maintaining energy reliability."
climate experts agree gas must swiftly curbed avert climate catastrophe.
Spokespeople Atmos Nicor did directly respond questions scientific consensus.
emailed comment, representative Colorado' Greeley-Weld Habitat affiliate said: " long constructed homes variety energy sources, including natural gas electricity."
Daniel Aldana Cohen, co-director progressive thinktank Climate Community Project, said gas-powered ZNE projects made unnecessary compromise: they tie access affordable housing increased planet-heating pollution.
" two great existential crises most people face eviction climate breakdown, second one making first one worse," said Aldana Cohen, whose research informed federal Green New Deal Public Housing proposals.
Health concerns
Experts long warned gas appliances can emit pollutants, including carcinogens. recent years, slew studies linked gas stoves increased risk childhood asthma, chronic lung disease other health impacts.
Low-income people people color may higher risk if exposed these pollutants, they likely asthma other underlying respiratory issues due disproportionate exposure environmental contaminants.
Gas trade groups note recent World Health Organization-funded study found gas stoves had non-significant impact childhood asthma. same study noted connection between gas use pneumonia lower respiratory infections.
Last, US regulators suggested potential eventual nationwide gas stove ban, though Biden later ruled out such policy.
Yet utilities behind partnerships repeatedly referred gas "clean". during dedication ceremony zero-net energy home built Habitat affiliate Atmos Lafayette, Louisiana, representative utility claimed air quality home would " best quality breathing air you can ".
Spokespeople Atmos Nicor did respond inquiries whether ZNE homes included gas stoves. Atmos representative December 2023 industry webinar said there "natural gas cooking these homes, every single one them".
Asked concerns gas appliances can degrade indoor air quality, representative Atmos Energy directed Guardian claims made American Gas Association, major trade group private gas utilities.

choice "combine renewables", representative says, "kind great talking point 'hey, partnering renewables, against them.'"

#80
Some world' most profitable - most polluting corporations - invested carbon offset projects fundamental failings "probably junk", suggesting industry claims greenhouse gas reductions likely overblown, according new analysis.
Delta, Gucci, Volkswagen, ExxonMobil, Disney, easyJet Nestlé among major corporations purchased millions carbon credits climate friendly projects "likely junk" or worthless when comes offsetting greenhouse gas emissions, according classification system developed Corporate Accountability, non-profit, transnational corporate watchdog.
Some these companies no longer use CO2 offsets amid mounting evidence carbon trading do lead claimed emissions cuts - some cases may even cause environmental social harms.
However, multibillion-dollar voluntary carbon trading industry still championed many corporations including oil gas majors, airlines, automakers, tourism, fast-food beverage brands, fashion houses, banks tech firms bedrock climate action - way claiming reduce greenhouse gas footprint while continuing rely fossil fuels unsustainable supply chains.
Yet, 33 top 50 corporate buyers, third entire offsets portfolio "likely junk" - suggesting least some claims carbon neutrality emission reductions exaggerated according analysis. fundamental failings leading "likely junk" ranking include whether emissions cuts would happened anyway, often case large hydroelectric dams, or if emissions just shifted elsewhere, common issue forestry offset projects.
"These findings add mounting evidence peels back greenwashed facade voluntary carbon market lays bare ways dangerously distracts real, lasting action world' largest corporations polluters need taking," said Rachel Rose Jackson, Corporate Accountability' director research.
fossil fuel industry far largest investor world' most popular 50 CO2 offsetting schemes. least 43% 81m CO2 credits purchased oil gas majors projects least one fundamental flaw "probably junk", according analysis.
transport industry, accounts fifth all global planet-warming emissions, relied heavily carbon offsetting projects meet climate goals. Just 42% total credits (55m) purchased airlines 38% purchased automakers (21m) top 50 projects likely worthless reducing emissions, analysis found.
new analysis, shared Guardian, builds joint investigation last into top 50 CO2 offset projects - those sold most carbon credits global market. vast majority most popular 50 offset projects classified likely or potentially junk due one or fundamental failing undermines promised emission cut, according criteria classification system applied analyses.
top 50 projects include forestry schemes, hydroelectric dams, solar wind farms, waste disposal greener household appliances schemes across 20 (mostly) developing countries, according data AlliedOffsets, most comprehensive emissions trading database, tracks projects inception. They account almost third entire global voluntary carbon market (VCM), suggesting junk or overvalued carbon credits exaggerate emission reduction benefits norm.
VCM industry works carbon credits being tradable "allowances" or certificates allows purchaser offset 1 ton carbon dioxide or equivalent greenhouse gasses investing environmental projects anywhere world claim reduce carbon emissions.
Climate experts say carbon trading market failed produce promised planetary benefits, delayed transition away oil, gas coal, caused harm forests communities developing countries where most offset projects located.

" report documents prevalence 'worthless' or 'likely junk' carbon offsets global Voluntary Carbon Market, undermines corporate rationale claiming emissions reductions based such credits," Heede added.

Last month, expert UN assessment warned invasive species had become multibillion-dollar problem, expected worsen without action conservation. least 3,500 harmful invasive species recorded globally, spread human travel trade, they playing increased role natural disasters such August' deadly wildfires Hawaii.

" entire ecosystem change without intensification control efforts."

buildup plastics, chemicals, pesticides fertilisers natural ecosystems highlighted threat biodiversity demands immediate action, experts say. Dr Marcus Eriksen, co-founder 5 Gyres Institute, works reduce plastic pollution, said action vital avoid flooding key ecosystems waste.

Federico Maggi University Sydney, said: " world' crops fields, 3m tonnes pesticides used every control unwanted plants, fungi bacteria. amount applied fields, 82% biodegraded simpler molecules currently fully characterised environmental biodiversity effects. remainder, 10% stays soil, while 8% leaches aquifers."
pesticide residues remain maintain essential function, said, "hence reducing biodiversity wherever they transported... reducing earthworm populations, amphibians, pollinators many other non-target organisms".
James Dalton, director IUCN global water programme, said impact human pollution being seen underground, world' groundwater. " use [water] often don't put back where came, meaning don't recharge groundwater any excess water took out didn't use," said.
" water use, pollute, some those pollutants go back into ground. then slowly infiltrates into our future water supply - sometimes permanently. US, they polluted groundwater can never cleaned (think Erin Brockovich).
" future holds difficult choices," said. called : "far better regulation groundwater use; far, far stricter controls pollutants monitoring pollutants; leaving some areas planet free development land protect water resources underneath."
Sri Lankan navy personnel try clear 'nurdles' - tiny plastic pellets used industry - washed ashore crippled cargo vessel off Colombo 2021. UN report called 'single largest plastic spill' history. Photograph: Chamila Karunarathne/EPA
'Huge shift marine ecosystems'
While climate change poses direct threat humanity, presents major threat swathes life Earth, often unforeseen ways, warn researchers. Henry Häkkinen, post-doctoral fellow ZSL' Institute Zoology, said: "Seabirds Europe already face lot problems - invasive predators, entanglement fishing gear, avian influenza severely threaten populations, among many other threats. now our seas warming, causing huge shift how marine ecosystems function. Many seabirds rely these cold-water species, especially during breeding season, if food disappears, so too seabirds."
Juan Lucas Restrepo, director general Alliance Bioversity International, warned climate crisis present major challenge future food supplies south Asia. "Climate change continue major driver changes [south Asian] ecosystems coming decades.
"Rising temperatures, prolonged droughts extreme climate events already modifying natural habitat where many crop species grow... negative impacts yields food security.
" huge threat environmental, social economic sustainability region. If trend continues, limit food availability increase food prices, resulting rising undernourishment."
Susan Chomba, director vital landscapes Africa WRI, said navigating threats posed climate crisis Africa ecosystems would international consequences. " world can't solve hunger crisis, nor climate crisis, without Africa.

" today facing perfect storm: almost 60% continent' arable land degraded. 280 million Africans face hunger. Climate-driven droughts cyclones wiping out development gains made past decades. Staying path hurt countries' economies decimate one our biggest carbon sinks, Congo basin."
Chomba concluded: "Hope lies 33 million smallholder farmers who produce 70% food continent export. Niger Kenya, these farmers making major leaps, restoring degraded land into productive farms grow healthy foods rich biodiversity major carbon sinks. Throughout Africa, seeing hope new path."

absence funds mend these units, they often demolished or privatized instead. result, US public housing stock shrunk 1.2m units just 900,000 between 2009 2022 - 25% decline - according analysis progressive thinktank Climate Community Project, informed policy proposal.

help create supply efficient technologies all residents. Construction start before new gas pipeline expansion through Pacific north-west state officials say undermine region' renewable transition further fuel climate emergencies.
region suffering annual wildfires, deadly heat domes drought. Lawmakers Washington, Oregon California passed some country' most stringent laws move away fossil fuels, they say federal commission greenlit project threatens undermine progress.
"Our state, whole west coast, numerous laws restrict appetite gas years come," said Jay Inslee, governor Washington state, who signed law 2021 set caps state' largest emitters. " they essentially ignored."
October, Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (Ferc) approved expansion GTN Xpress, pipeline carries gas fracking fields Western Canada through Idaho, Washington, Oregon California. Proponents say expansion needed meet growing consumer demand.
Ferc' own draft environmental assessment found expansion would result greenhouse gas equivalent adding 700,000 gasoline-powered vehicles road each.
expansion would upgrade compressor stations, allowing gas pass through existing pipeline. Critics say upgrading compressor stations helps GTN Xpress bypass some regulations.
" thought right path," said Pam Marsh, Oregon state representative, who sponsored 2021 law requiring state utilities transition carbon-free electricity generation 2040. 2020, wildfire destroyed 2,500 homes Marsh' district. "Climate change happening, it' right front us, it' devastating, do everything can mitigate potential damage yet come," she said.
November, attorneys general Washington, Oregon California environmental groups filed joint petition asking Ferc withdraw "deficient, unlawful order authorizing GTN Xpress Project", arguing Ferc failed critically assess project' climate impacts. They said plan did account state laws would decrease gas demand. Ferc until 22 December respond.
"Ferc completely captured agency failing do responsibility under law," said Jeff Merkley, Oregon senator. " mind, 'need' simply means fossil fuel companies want do, so they rubber-stamp."
Ferc independent agency, collects annual fees industries regulates, including gas industry. last two decades, commission approved 423 425 pipeline projects came before.
Merkley three other senators urged commission reject proposed expansion, arguing "if GTN continues business usual pipeline 2050, would represent 48 percent region' target GHG emissions all sources". They added, "Put simply, there no way our states can meet emissions goals if project moves forward."
Opponents voiced concerns health risks associated expansion. Compressor stations emit cocktail pollutants, like benzene, linked higher risk some cancers, carbon monoxide.
Lawmakers environmental groups criticized environmental safety human rights record TC Energy, GTN' parent-company; fined environmental violations pipeline British Columbia, where accused working police arrest Indigenous demonstrators. There explosion Virginia gas pipeline huge oil spill Keystone pipeline 2022.
Indigenous groups, including Indigenous Environmental Network Columbia River Intertribal Fish Commission, say project contributes climate crisis puts native species, like steelhead trout salmon, risk.
" definitely worried [ expansion]," said Alysia Aguilar Littleleaf, who runs fly fishing business Warm Springs reservation Oregon. "That' why opposed very get-go."
When reached comment, Michael Tadeo, TC Energy spokesperson, wrote: "Having undergone two years analysis consideration agency, GTN XPress one most thoroughly reviewed infrastructure projects approved FERC." added, " appreciate FERC' bipartisan action approve project work diligently place into service soon possible." Tadeo did respond questions TC Energy' environmental record.
Opponents project argue expanding pipeline assumes demand fossil fuels stays present-day levels or grows time. However, advocates argue consumer demand fall energy transition progresses.
"Those precedent agreements do paint full picture demand," said Audrey Leonard, lawyer Columbia Riverkeeper, fighting project. "Just because you someone willing buy gas doesn't mean gas necessary."
state regulator independently came same conclusion., Washington' regulatory commission ridiculed utility Cascade, contract buy gas GTN Xpress, relying old energy consumption models fail consider new state laws. called Cascade' analysis GTN Xpress "conspicuously inadequate".
TC Energy Cascade did respond questions state commission' comments.
Asked if project needed, Tadeo wrote email demand gas existing GTN system grew 26% 2014 2021, while supply constrained, leading "unnecessarily high energy prices strained reliability consumers".
If Ferc denies petition stop project, TC Energy can begin construction, although opponents probably appeal through federal courts.
" intend enforce our laws," Inslee said. "If you do construction, enforce them, you'll stranded asset, you'll bunch compressors sitting there pipeline you can't use you made ratepayers pay."

increase UK chargers during 2023 almost certainly outstripped growth electric car sales, 28% November compared previous 12 months.

Countries should move coal renewable energy without shifting gas "transition" fuel save money, high gas prices market volatility made fossil fuel expensive option, analysis found.
Natural gas long touted "transition" fuel economies dependent coal power needs, lower carbon dioxide emissions coal requires similar centralised infrastructure, gas-fired power stations take only couple years build. Earlier, before Russia invaded Ukraine, European Commission angered green campaigners including gas "bridge" clean energy guidebook green investment.
High prices gas, plummeting cost renewable energies such wind solar power, reversed logic, according analysis TransitionZero. cost switching coal renewable energy plunged 99% since 2010, according report published Tuesday.
findings call into question economic viability scores gas coal-fired power plants planned or under construction around world. 615GW new gas plants, 442GW new coal-fired power stations, planned construction globally, according analysis.
Matt Gray, co-founder TransitionZero, said no longer made sense think gas transition fuel, countries should opt renewables place coal. India facing looming power crisis, stocks coal power plants fallen unprecedentedly low levels states warning power blackouts.
States across India issued panicked warnings coal supplies thermal power plants, convert heat coal electricity, running perilously low.
According data Central Electricity Authority India, nearly 80% country' coal-fired plants critical, or "supercritical" stage, meaning stocks run out less five days.
weekend, Delhi' chief minister, Arvind Kejriwal, wrote prime minister, Narendra Modi, capital " face blackout" if power stations did receive coal.
States including Rajasthan, Jharkhand Bihar experiencing power cuts lasting 14 hours.
Maharashtra shut down 13 thermal power plants urged people use electricity sparingly, Punjab three power plants halted production. Scheduled power cuts introduced Punjab, lasting six hours time, prompted protests.
However, experts emphasised power issues due shortage domestic coal production, some reported.
past two decades, domestic coal production India continued rise exponentially, though there minor dip production less 1% 2019 2020 due pandemic.
Energy providers India' state-owned coal producer, Coal India Limited, instead accused failing stockpile sufficient amounts meet predicted rise demand.
" current crisis manifested shortage coal mining capacity, instead caused due improper foresight, planning stocking coal power generators energy regulator country," said Sunil Dahiya, analyst Centre Research Energy Clean Air.
year' heavy monsoon rains blamed affecting domestic coal mining due flooding impeding dispatch coal mines.
While no different every, coal usually imported bridge gap production. due global energy crisis, seen international prices hit record highs, financial challenge import coal, leading greater shortages usual.
Tuesday government asked power producers import 10% necessary coal stocks warned states federal companies curb power supplies if they found selling electricity power exchanges cash surging prices.
Vibhuti Garg, lead India economist Institute Energy Economics Financial Analysis, said there had significant growth electricity demand recently, India had re-emerged Covid lockdown, added had anticipated months ago, so should taken power companies surprise.
"Various stakeholders blamed situation," said Garg, who described situation "scary likely get better soon".
"Developers playing safe, thinking there high chance another Covid wave hitting around time would suppress demand. So they didn't do enough stock coal end.
"So, while there availability coal last few months, developers maintaining enough stock end, leading crisis situation now."
Garg said past "renewable energy installation slowed down", if Indian government had focused increasing capacity renewables such solar, wind hydro, " increased demand met through renewable energy crisis averted".
Seeking allay fears around power shortages, India' minister power, RK Singh, said "panic unnecessarily created coal shortages".
Coal minister Pralhad Joshi blamed heavy rains high price international coal disrupting supply insisted "another three four days, things alright".
However, there concerns shortages used justify pushing domestic coal production, some state governments already putting pressure expedited clearance new coalmines protected areas.
India already plans boost domestic coal production 1bn tons 2024.
" situation used few stakeholders push forward opening coalmines citing reason low coal availability that' true all," said Dahiya.
Any further expansion mostly affect India' tribal communities who live forests cover India' largest coal reserves, who already born brunt environmental damage coal mining.
If India meet climate targets coming years, country need wean itself off coal, currently powers almost 70% electricity.
"I think government using opportunity push coal mining or coal-based power generation," said Garg. " should avoid investing money assets become stranded.
"Coal based generation expensive prices further increase. other hand, renewable energy generation cheap prices likely go down further."

American private equity tycoons profiteering global climate crisis investing fossil fuels driving greenhouse gas emissions, new investigation reveals.
Oil gas pipelines, coal plants offshore drilling sites linked Indigenous land violations, toxic leaks deadly air pollution among dirty energy projects financed some country' largest private equity firms, according investigation corporate accountability non-profits LittleSis Private Equity Stakeholder Project (Pesp).
Private equity refers opaque form financing away public markets funds investors buy restructure companies including startups, troubled businesses real estate.
Globally, industry manages $7tn wealthy individuals institutional investors such mutuals, hedge funds, endowments pensions, investing every sector retail chains healthcare prisons weapons.
Some money finances fossil-fuel projects release greenhouse gases cause global heating. Higher atmospheric ocean temperatures directly linked rise catastrophic weather events like drought, extreme temperatures hurricanes.
Yet unlike banks other publicly listed companies, private equity firms exempt most financial disclosure rules, making extremely difficult track assets.
means people like firefighters teachers whose pension funds invested private equity funds little way knowing if retirement nest egg financing coal plants, oil wells or solar farms.
Private Equity' Dirty Dozen report, shared exclusively Guardian, provides snapshot industry' involvement some country' most controversial fossil fuel investments, well deep political cultural ties wealthy executives. investigation found:
* Carlyle Group, one world' largest private equity firms, owns dozens oil gas companies including stake NGP Energy Capital, boasts own major portfolio mostly focused fracking drilling states like Texas, Wyoming Colorado. national electricity market reached new milestone Sunday, solar power outstripping energy generation coal first time since market set two decades ago.
crossover point lasted only few minutes, low demand sunny skies Sunday meant contribution coal dropped record low 9,315MW just after noon, while solar provided dominant share 9,427MW.
Dylan McConnell, research fellow University Melbourne' climate energy college, said brief moment renewable energy represented 57% national electricity generation.
" what I unofficially call 'record season'," McConnell said. "It' actually still pretty early season [ get these numbers] spring or shoulder seasons you combination low demand, because there' no heating or cooling, then nice weather weekend.



"Those factors combine, you get these giant shares renewable energy generally push out coal."
While McConnell said only "fleeting" "Australia long way peak renewable energy", energy prices went negative Sunday 8.30am through 5pm.

Why price electricity pegged gas?
Typically, electricity prices reflect gas prices. because types energy needed produce electricity UK ranked, renewables nuclear prioritised ahead coal gas.
price electricity set every half hour margin cost last generating unit switched off meet demand, typically gas. pushes electricity prices when wholesale gas prices rise.
What energy contract difference?
Under these contracts, electricity plants paid flat rate what they produce 15 years.
rate difference between "strike price" - agreed price reflects investment green technology - "reference price", reflection wholesale market price electricity UK.
If market price higher strike price, Low Carbon Contracts Company, wholly owned government, ensures these savings passed consumer bills. If market price lower, LCCC makes shortfall.
government uses system, introduced 2017, support development low-carbon electricity generation projects high upfront costs long lifetimes protect developers volatile wholesale prices.
However, only 15% total fleet renewable power projects CfD; most remunerated through older Renewables Obligation Certificate payments.
What renewables obligation certificates?
Under renewables obligation model, designed wind solar, power generators receive value electricity wholesale market, well additional subsidy. Therefore, generators these contracts benefited soaring gas prices so far.

Under energy savings plan, all EU member states strive reduce gas consumption 15% August through end March. event total shutdown Russian gas or high demand, EU states can declare energy emergency triggers immediate mandatory savings. Member states rejected attempt European Commission decide when energy emergency under way, would launch compulsory energy rationing.

European Commission accused undermining climate goals after defied critics pushing ahead plans include gas nuclear EU guidebook "green" investments.
Gas nuclear deemed bridge technologies meet EU' target net zero emissions 2050, long-awaited proposals EU' "taxonomy environmentally sustainable economic activities", published Wednesday.
" reason including gas nuclear way doing because firmly believe recognises need these energy sources transition," EU commissioner financial services, Mairead McGuinness, told reporters.
Critics said including gas nuclear guide intended prevent greenwashing jeopardised EU' climate goals hopes keeping global heating below 1.5C.
" European Commission significantly undermining EU' credibility climate actor," Bas Eickhout, Dutch Green MEP vice-chair European parliament' environment committee, said. " UN climate summit Glasgow, small steps taken towards phasing out fossil fuels. Yet, unfortunately, commission already turning back clock leaving door open gas industry."
Laurence Tubiana, chief executive European Climate Foundation architect landmark 2015 Paris climate agreement, said: " EU taxonomy envisioned vital tool align financial flows Paris agreement. Instead, Europe undermining climate leadership lowering standards EU beyond. When gold standard does emerge elsewhere, taxonomy left behind."
Since proposals leaked New Year' Eve 2021 - fuelling bitter accusations lack transparency - commission made minor tweaks make easier gas projects get green guidebook.
Gas plants can get stamp approval if they switch low-carbon gases or renewables 2035. previous requirement phase- low-carbon fuels 2026 dropped.
taxonomy - intended channel billions private money into climate-friendly investments - fast becoming one biggest controversies Ursula von der Leyen' tenure European Commission president. Last month Greta Thunberg climate activists slammed plans "fake climate action" flout scientific advice.
further sign anger, campaign group Avaaz staged mock burial taxonomy roundabout outside commission headquarters, activists wearing face masks Von der Leyen, Germany' Olaf Scholz France' Emmanuel Macron. France pressured Von der Leyen grant stamp approval nuclear power, while Germany had lobbied inclusion gas, although Scholz' coalition government split issue.
"Europe witnessing our biggest setback yet our moonshot mission," said Patricia Martín Díaz Avaaz. "Labelling fossil gas nuclear green incompatible EU' 2050 climate targets our hope keeping 1.5C alive."
Institutional Investors Group Climate Change, represents 375 funds holding €51tn assets under management, urged commission drop gas, citing International Energy Agency studies show Europe' existing gas plants close 2035.?? "Put simply, there no remaining carbon budget new investments natural gas," group said.

Other critics include expert panel convened commission, said plans " line" original regulation, agreed July 2020. stinging rebuke, EU platform sustainable finance - group includes industry, NGO finance experts EU institutions - said they had doubts criteria gas nuclear, while "many deeply concerned environmental impacts may result".
group said only gas plants emitting less 100 grams CO2 equivalent per kilowatt hour lifecycle should allowed, criteria excludes conventional gas. contrast, commission would allow gas plants emitting 270g CO2e/kWh classed "sustainable" until 2030. second alternative would allow gas plants emit average 550g CO2 e/kWh twenty- lifespan. Critics describe loophole allows new gas plants built promise carbon capture technology yet take off.
EU taxonomy became law July 2020, legislators left important details resolved through so-called delegated acts - secondary legislation meant technical issues subject same degree ministerial parliamentary oversight.
Critics accused commission abusing process, smuggling controversial issues nuclear gas into latest delegated act, rather drafting separate law.
Only supermajority 20 out 27 EU member states, or majority European parliament' 705 MEPs can now defeat plans during scrutiny period four six months began Wednesday.
Commission officials played down threat legal challenge Austria Luxembourg, describing " very theoretical discussion". Both countries oppose nuclear power, while Green ministers German coalition government dismissed plans greenwashing.
Denmark, Sweden Netherlands had urged commission label gas green, while Germany declared opposition nuclear.
Yet France, backed central eastern European member states, lobbied Von der Leyen include gas nuclear, they consider bridge EU' net zero future.
largest group European parliament, European People' party come out favour gas, underscoring difficulties defeating plans. "Flexibility needed way decarbonise. If, example, Poland would like replace coal plant several small gas plants, should possible if helps achieve climate neutrality", Esther de Lange, Dutch Christian Democrat, said.
Lisa Fischer, who leads E3G thinktank' work climate neutral energy systems, said gas nuclear had no place taxonomy. "Gas investments only harmful climate they increasingly financially risky. Nuclear makes EU' energy transition costly needs."
sign intense arguments, McGuinness revealed had no unanimity among commission' top 27 officials; she said "overwhelming majority" EU commissioners had backed plans.
" legally obliged do," she said referring back initial July 2020 law. Opponents national capitals, however, say commission had no obligation include gas or nuclear.

Energy efficiency measures already saved average British household £1,000 energy bills, further insulation home improvements halve future bills, analysis shown.
future savings unlikely realised unless government focuses swiftly insulation, savings date come largely efficiency improvements electrical appliances boilers, repeated.
energy price cap forecast rise £2,000 typical household April, owing gas crisis, £1,300 today. average household energy bills would £3,000 if range regulatory measures brought down energy use last two decades, according Regulatory Assistance Project (RAP), analyst organisation.
Jan Rosenow, director European programmes RAP, said analysis showed failures government past decade take action would staved off much current energy price crisis. "If government had acted, had properly funded programme would reduced greenhouse gases energy bills," said.

Electrical appliances such lightbulbs, fridges washing machines now use much less power 20 years ago, owing EU directives. 2005 UK government regulation mandating use condensing boilers brought down average gas use significantly.
Energy consumption overall declined 16% since 2000 despite 15% increase number homes, average home being 10% larger rise appliance ownership, RAP said.
Insulation, double glazing similar home upgrades somewhat reduced energy use, Rosenow said, far less possible, UK fallen behind such improvements. Insulation rates plummeted past decade, after successive government schemes scrapped replaced.
least 14m households missed out insulation because abandonment green deal scheme, set 2013 stopped 2015, only 15,000 homes upgraded. further 47,500 improved most recent successor scheme, green homes grant, instead 600,000 promised, when scheme scrapped after only six months.
"stop-start" nature government' insulation efforts stifled growth insulation industry, requires skilled workers broad network suppliers, Rosenow added. Thousands jobs lost industry past decade.
RAP analysis suggested would mistake cut energy company obligation (ECO), government considering. £1bn programme, cost added energy bills, channels funding insulation people low incomes. Cutting ECO "would slow down much-needed transformation our housing stock leave people exposed rising energy prices future", RAP said.
RAP findings tally separate estimates Carbon Brief cutting insulation programmes past 10 years added £1bn UK' energy bills.
Rosenow said failure keep consistent action insulation massive missed opportunity, action now still reduce bills future years. Some analysts forecast high gas prices least next two years, world recovers coronavirus pandemic economic shocks.
"Gas boilers now efficient they can. big area improvement insulation," said Rosenow. "You can't get net zero without insulation. It' impossible, would far too expensive impractical."

"Government plans energy company obligation include increasing value scheme £1bn per annum extending 2022 2026, set out our fuel poverty strategy."

memo claimed internal BP documents highlighted how carbon capture storage (CCS) - nascent technology involves inserting CO2 emissions into underground rock formations - "enable full use fossil fuels across energy transition beyond".

committee said internal Shell messaging guidance - developed "insulate Shell" lawsuits "greenwashing" "misleading investors" climate crisis - calls employees emphasise net zero emissions " collective ambition world" rather "Shell goal or target".
guidance tells employees: "Please do give impression Shell willing reduce carbon dioxide emissions levels do make business sense."
