added: "Failure maintain [supplies] would shatter Russia' reputation reliable partner would tectonic shift. Russia would seen threat would far-reaching consequences European economies, even consequences Russia. Europe would choose strategy diverge Russia."
What impact Russia' actions Ukraine climate diplomacy?
Russia almost invisible Cop26 UN climate summit Glasgow last November, after surprising public commitment reach net zero greenhouse gas emissions 2060. Kremlin left details goal vague shown little sign updating Russia' emissions commitments 2030, key limiting global temperature rises 1.5C.
crucial climate diplomacy, pledges all countries Glasgow still inadequate 1.5C goal, UK Cop26 presidency hoping major progress before next UN climate meeting Egypt November. Russia' stance Ukraine certainly impact those discussions, least any conflict focus attention move fossil fuels.
notable Russia' oil gas operations among worst world climate. Data released week IEA shows emissions methane, powerful greenhouse gas major component natural gas, 70% higher countries had claimed.
Russia makes little or no attempt capture methane oil gas drilling, stop pipelines leaking, so there huge opportunities. first time world position limit global heating under 2C, according first -depth analysis net zero pledges made nations UN Cop26 climate summit December.
Before these pledges likely peak climate crisis there would temperature rise above 2C, bringing severe impacts billions people. Now likely peak temperature rise 1.9C.
However, researchers said depended all nations implementing pledges time full, warned policies do so place. pledges include those developing countries said happen without financial technical support.
Achieving pledges needed 2C limit "historic milestone" good news, scientists said. However, they said bad news cuts global emissions currently planned 2030 way off track keep peak below 1.5C. global goal, currently there less 10% chance hitting target.
People across planet already facing intensifying heatwaves, floods storms 1.2 C heating caused humanity' emissions date, Intergovernmental Panel Climate Change (IPCC) warned 2018 far worse if heating continues above 1.5C.
2C limit being within reach "big news", said Christophe McGlade International Energy Agency, member team behind new analysis. "It' first time governments come forward specific targets can hold global warming below symbolic 2C level."
"These results clearly cause optimism," added. "We've come long way since Paris agreement signed back 2015. now real work start. pledges yet backed strong credible near-term policies needed make them reality."
Prof Malte Meinshausen, University Melbourne, Australia, another team member, said having 2C limit sight "historic milestone".
said: "Our study clearly shows increased action decade necessary. Otherwise, going blast through remaining carbon budget 1.5C." major IPCC report earlier April said global emissions must peak start fall within 30 months keep 1.5C target alive.
new analysis, published journal Nature, first peer-reviewed study assess peak temperature rise would result countries fulfilling pledges. used two independent modelling approaches, one assessed 1,400 different scenarios included recent pledges shipping aviation emissions.
end Cop26, 153 nations had submitted new climate pledges UN, countries responsible 75% global greenhouse gas emissions committing reach net zero between 2050 2070. These made 2C limit possibility, though scientists warned uncertainties how planet responds rising emissions meant there remained 5% chance temperature above 2.8C.
climate policies actually place today would mean peak 2.6C, leading "massive climate damages around world", said McGlade. commitments made countries so far 2030 only cut peak 2.4C. IPCC said limiting heating 1.5C requires reducing CO2 emissions 45% 2030 compared 2010.
emissions track rise 7-15% 2030, "sobering assessment", scientists said, any delay action putting 1.5C "out reach". If world does overshoot target, then ensuring "livable future" would rely massive rollout technology can suck CO2 air, well large-scale reforestation.
new research gave much clearer picture our likely climate future, said Frances Moore, University California, Zeke Hausfather, climate research lead Stripe, commentary Nature. They said showed goal ratcheting initial climate pledges made 2015 Paris had "partially realised".
" optimism should curbed until promises reduce emissions future backed stronger short-term action," they said. " easy set ambitious climate targets 30, 40 or even 50 years future, much harder enact policies [needed] today."
Moore Hausfather warned danger geopolitical tensions, including Russia' war Ukraine: " would mistake rule out future characterised resurgent nationalism strains global cooperation leads increasing reliance domestic fossil-fuel resources corresponding rise emissions."
"Policymakers standing crossroads," said McGlade. " can choose lock emissions deepen energy crisis. Or can use moment take honest step towards cleaner, safer future."
McGlade said there many policies make immediate or near-term impacts energy climate crises, including reducing speed limits roads, accelerating rollout renewable energy electric vehicles, stopping venting methane, powerful greenhouse gas, oil gas production facilities putting into supply instead.

Big oil gas companies spending tens millions publicising environmental work, while only 10th investment goes into low-carbon development, report claims.
comprehensive study public communications five oil gas firms InfluenceMap, climate finance thinktank, found 60% publicity made least one claim highlighting companies' positive climate actions. average, five companies devoted only 12% capital expenditure low-carbon activities - included some gas projects.
Less quarter publicity material highlighted companies' fossil fuel activities, InfluenceMap said, suggested companies spending $750m communications aimed burnishing climate credentials.
Researchers looked 3,421 public communications materials published BP, Chevron, ExxonMobil, Shell TotalEnergies 2021, including articles blogposts corporate websites, press releases, reports, speeches company CEO social media accounts.
researchers did look adverts possible them obtain full dataset any company' global advertising
They found 60% publicity material made least one green claim, most popular being centred efforts "transition energy mix". However, analysis capital expenditure five companies found all forecast increase oil gas production, exception BP, expected similar levels 2026 2021.
"Essentially, found big oil spending millions dollars green PR, really systematic campaign portray themselves pro-climate," said Faye Holder, programme manager InfluenceMap. " same time, they still lobbying lock fossil fuels investing really unsustainable energy future high levels oil gas, very low spend low-carbon activities."
None " us" pages firms' websites described them oil gas companies, Holder said. " best instance, my mind, BP - 'Who are' page, they only mention word 'oil' twice. it' bottom page, under section called 'Our history', where they describe how they always transitioning energy company, coal oil gas lower-carbon future.
"So it' really clear they want dissociate themselves oil gas, attach themselves climate agenda."
Shell made most green claims, 70% public communications stressing pro-environmental activities, while just 10% capital expenditure invested low carbon, included some gas projects, according InfluenceMap' report. ExxonMobil made green claims 70% communications, while devoting 8% capital expenditure low carbon. TotalEnergies, 62% communications made green claims, 25% capital investment going towards low carbon.
Shell contested findings, saying InfluenceMap had failed take into account low-carbon businesses included marketing division, including EV charging low-carbon fuels, joint venture Brazil world-leading bioethanol producer. company previously said 35% capital expenditure 2022 would go towards low-carbon energy, well "non-energy products".
" already investing billions dollars lower-carbon energy," spokesperson said. " help alter mix energy Shell sells, need grow these new businesses rapidly. means letting our customers know through advertising or social media what lower-carbon solutions offer now or developing, so they can switch when time right them.
" world still need oil gas many years come. Investment them ensure can supply energy people still rely, while lower-carbon alternatives scaled."
spokeperson TotalEnergies told Guardian: "Our public announcements policy reflects transformation TotalEnergies multi-energy company.
" evidence, InfluenceMap report releases forecast ranks TotalEnergies frontrunner among supermajors terms renewables assets-based capacities."
spokesperson ExxonMobil said: "ExxonMobil continues mitigate emissions operations achieved 2025 emission-reduction plans four years earlier planned.
" progress supports company' aggressive 2030 emission-reduction plans ambition achieve net zero scope 1 2 greenhouse gas emissions operated assets 2050. ExxonMobil investing $15bn between now 2027 lower-emission initiatives, anticipate tripling investment 2025."
Chevron BP did respond requests comment.?271

Queensland end reliance coal-fired power 2035 under 10- $62bn energy plan create clean "super-grid" solar, wind hydroelectric power.
historic announcement state known coal mining, premier, Annastacia Palaszczuk, said Queensland facing "climate emergency" bold vision needed.
Palaszczuk said her government committing extra $4bn transform state' energy system 2030 there would least 2,000 wind turbines 35m solar panels state.
2035, there would eight times renewable energy generation today.
speech Brisbane, she said: "It' changing game. Action climate change. Action our economy. Action can all take part action can all proud. Queensland - it' our time shine."
Two new targets would legislated, Palaszczuk said. 2032, 70% state' electricity would come renewables, rising 80% 2035.
She revealed state wanted build world' largest pumped hydro plant west Mackay, 5GW round--clock storage, first stage completed 2032.
Workers state' eight publicly-owned coal-fired power plants would protected $150m "job security guarantee" either keep working those sites or find new careers.
Coal plants would turned into clean energy hubs, 2035, state would no longer rely them electricity. Projections showed coal providing no power all after 2037.
Government private investment $62bn estimated needed, power generation being majority publicly-owned.
Household annual electricity costs would $150 less under plan, government said.
state' second preferred pumped hydro project, west Mackay, would known Pioneer-Burdekin.
Another pumped hydro plant, announced earlier, being targeted site near Imbil west Sunshine Coast.
deputy premier, Steven Miles, said two plants combined would bigger Snowy Mountains hydroelectric scheme.
" use cheap solar electricity during day pump water mountain store. Then night can release water generate electricity," Miles said.
"It' like giant battery."
Palaszczuk pointed state' hosting Olympics Paralympics 2032, saying Queensland would deliver "climate positive" games.
Queensland energy renewables minister, Mick de Brenni, told Guardian state' move away coal marked historic moment.
" incredibly significant day our nation' history," said.
"Today Queensland commences transition orderly way so there' no reliance 2035 [ coal]."
plan' release comes only days after premier announced state investing $776m build country' biggest publicly-owned windfarm state' west.
Studies previously questioned whether Queensland track meet initial target 50% renewables 2030 - pace private investment stalled, government showed lack appetite hasten coal closures.
Maggie Mckeown, Queensland Conservation Council, said announcement " major turning point government" plan stronger earlier investment batteries.
"Six gigawatts 2035 great start," Mckeown said.

Hydrogen produced fossil fuels expensive, release greenhouse gas emissions comes greater risk creating stranded assets, according new research Australian National University.
paper, published peer-reviewed engineering journal Applied Energy, researchers compared emissions financial cost producing hydrogen using fossil fuels or renewable energy.
"Blue hydrogen" produced using natural gas while "green hydrogen" made running electric current through water using electrolyser powered renewable energy such wind or solar.

"Clean hydrogen" term used when carbon capture storage used capture carbon dioxide emissions during production process, similar proposals "clean coal".
Hydrogen become central Australian government' current proposal reach net zero, blue hydrogen endorsed Australia' former chief scientist Dr Alan Finkel way build industrial capability.
ANU researchers found emissions hydrogen made fossil fuels would still "substantial".
Researchers found current emissions estimates CCS fail account fugitive emissions such methane - potent greenhouse gas leaks into atmosphere during extraction natural gas.
These emissions caught CCS because creating hydrogen natural gas totally efficient - takes gas make hydrogen energy would simply burn gas - methane emissions continue grow rate extraction.
rate extraction grows supply export markets, so these emissions.
researchers found financial cost creating blue hydrogen using CCS becomes expensive plant gets closer capturing 90% emissions. because becomes harder capture CO2 concentrations begin fall.
Dr Fiona Beck, co-author report engineer ANU Institute Climate, Energy Disaster Solutions, said CCS requires expensive "bespoke solution every plant" adds risk these projects may become stranded assets.
"Green hydrogen expensive right now capacity very quickly reduce cost," Beck said. "Unless some form incentive people apply CCS, it' never going make sense make blue hydrogen."
" does beg question who' going invest blue hydrogen?"
high capture rates researchers put cost producing blue hydrogen $2.87 (US$2.09) kilogram per tonne CO2, while cost producing green hydrogen $4.99 (US$3.64) projections may come down $2.55 (US$1.86).
technology create blue hydrogen already around some time, it' unlikely there significant technological advance would drive down price, happened production solar panels.
CSIRO database tracking new hydrogen projects announced or under development records least 65 new green hydrogen projects Australia, compared just three trying create hydrogen fossil fuels CCS.
ANU research report supported other worldwide studies such one published September researchers working French energy giant Engie who found already possible produce green hydrogen similar prices blue hydrogen.
study found cost producing hydrogen fossil fuels between $1.34 $2.40 kilogram per tonne CO2, while already possible some operations produce hydrogen made solar power $2.36 kilogram.
Scott Hamilton, senior adviser Smart Energy Council Hydrogen Australia, said industry "long, long way" achieving these costs across board Australian government actively support develop green hydrogen.
" Australian government needs do hopes prayers magical thinking modelling," Hamilton said. "They need proper targets, proper policy actions bring these emerging technologies market, they did wind solar 20 years ago."
Hamilton said government currently under-pricing blue hydrogen net zero modelling assuming price hydrogen produced fossil fuels $1.80 kilogram, hydrogen produced through renewable energy only becoming cost-competitive 2030 - despite price natural gas growing steadily since 2015.
Dr Madeline Taylor, senior lecturer Macquarie University' school law climate councillor Climate Council Australia, said ANU research "confirmed business case, commercial viability case policy case" green hydrogen.
"There' lot money made there' lot risk if don't do right. If don't invest right technologies or send right signals," Taylor said.
"If don't think carefully, left stranded assets. left producing product world doesn't want. left infrastructure going fall upon taxpayer dismantle."

2000s, fossil fuel prices rising worldwide. After period volatility 1980s, crude oil price per barrel had reached one lowest points - $20 - end 2001 then, course six years, tripled before new oil shock saw prices surpass those 1970s, reaching record $145 barrel 3 July 2008.
Uruguay imports oil, so had problem. Demand energy country had grown 8.4% previous household energy bills increasing similar rate. 3.4 million-strong population becoming restless. Lacking alternatives, President Tabaré Vázquez forced buy energy neighbouring states higher prices, even though Argentina, Uruguay Paraguay had mutual aid agreement case emergency conditions.
escape trap, Vázquez needed rapid solutions. turned unlikely source: Ramón Méndez Galain, physicist who would transform country' energy grid into one cleanest world.
Today, country almost phased out fossil fuels electricity production. Depending weather, anything between 90% 95% power comes renewables. some years, number crept high 98%.
Phasing out fossil fuels central issue Cop28. After week tense negotiations Dubai, countries agreed transition away fossil fuels within energy systems - campaigners, governments environmental experts continue debate how make transition.
Physicist Ramón Méndez Galain: 'I thought I understand little problem.'
One answer lie what Uruguay achieved within decade half.
"I had working abroad 14 years, when I came back, there energy crisis, only solution people giving install nuclear power plant -," Galain remembers. "I nuclear physicist, so I thought I understand little problem."
Galain researched issue, became convinced nuclear power answer Uruguay. Instead, argued, renewables. published his findings paper laid out his belief country should go all wind power. Soon after, received phone call inviting him become Uruguay' energy secretary implement his plan.
"Imagine my surprise," Galain says. " crazy. I did something even crazy: I accepted."
Uruguay small country - 26% smaller UK area - wedged between two giants. Argentina' sprawling capital, Buenos Aires, lies 31 miles (50km) south mouth Rio de la Plata, forms part border between two countries, while Uruguay shares northern border Brazil.
context, country easily overlooked. However, economically South American success story. GDP per capita £16,420 2022, according World Bank, highest continent; only tiny fraction population lives extreme poverty. country burgeoning middle class - accounting 60% population - there high expectations lifestyle opportunities.
Such demographic change driven demand trappings contemporary, 21st-century lifestyle. Homes fitted washing machines dishwashers, air-conditioning units become commonplace, vast flat-screen TVs connected devices.
All requires power. course decade, Uruguay, under stewardship Galain, installed 50 windfarms across country, decarbonised grid bolstered hydropower.
biggest challenge, however, change "narrative" renewables. Back then, sustainable energies still surrounded many misconceptions, says Galain: they too expensive, too intermittent or would raise unemployment - changing these stories proved vital getting buy- all levels society.
"No one believed do. needed new solutions. needed do things differently," says. "Today, even members cabinet say me: 'When you saying those things TV 2008, thinking, how going explain when fail?'"
Galain says there needed "strong national narrative" make work. "I told people best option even if they don't believe climate change exists. It' cheapest dependent crazy fluctuations [ oil prices]."
narrative, government set winning sceptical populace. One initial concern jobs would lost energy sector. Instead, 50,000 new jobs created - large number country such small population. idea "just transition", nobody left behind, became central, some workers offered places retraining schemes adapt new normal.
Others able take advantage changes. Santiago Revello, 52, owns beef farm central Uruguay, 175 miles north capital, Montevideo. Beef production primary industry across country, owing vast swathes grasslands.
farm broke even financiallybut 2009, Revello family debating whether sell farm. when Revello met Fernando Schaich, who ran what previously small energy-efficiency consultancy, had learned country' transition renewables.
Schaich had spotted opportunity transition plan early. His company had previously made money advising businesses how they cut energy usage, moved into windfarm development. told Revello his family' farm site onshore windfarm - building one need affect his cattle.
pair prepared bid contract went developer, lost. "Fernando gutted," Revello says. " told me: 'You can go another company.' had always honest me, so I told him: 'It' all money.'"
Today, Revello' farm home 22 wind turbines, side business gives family healthy source income.

report, published partnership KPMG consultancy Kearney, found renewable energy sources - excluding hydro power - met just 7.5% world' energy demand last. represents increase nearly 1% previous, driven record growth wind solar energy. Solar generation climbed 25% 2022 while wind power output grew 13.5% compared before.
However, renewable energy boom eclipsed modest rise global energy consumption 1.1% last - compared 5.5% increase 2021 - meant oil coal burnt meet demand, report found.
Simon Virley, head energy natural resources KPMG, said: "Despite record growth renewables, share world energy still coming fossil fuels remains stubbornly stuck 82%, should act clarion call governments inject urgency into energy transition."
Global oil demand rose 2.9m barrels day last reach average 97.3m bpd 2022, part due return global economic activity after Covid pandemic, according Energy Institute.
same time, demand coal climbed highs seen since 2014, rising 0.6% compared 2021, driven demand India China, report said. appetite coal power increased line record high prices gas Europe Asia following Russia' invasion Ukraine. Gas made 24% world' energy use last, down 25% before, gas production remained relatively steady.
Energy Institute warned stubbornly high energy-related emissions risk derailing aims Paris climate agreement unless urgent action taken global governments, according report. Under Paris accord emissions should fall half end decade avoid triggering catastrophic levels global heating.
Richard Forrest, head global sustainability Kearney, said rise greenhouse gas emissions reinforced need "urgent action get world track meet Paris targets". added need deliver clean, affordable secure energy " never greater".

n 2019, France' best known climate expert sat down work most feted graphic novelist. result? Perhaps most terrifying comic ever drawn.
Part history, part analysis, part vision future, World Without End weaves story humanity' rapacious appetite fossil fuel energy, how made possible society people take granted, disastrous effects climate.

World Without End immediate hit French readers
Among French readers immediate smash hit, selling 1m copies so far, becoming country' top-selling book all categories 2022 hailed "one most brilliant summaries climate issues ever written".
controversial solutions provoked backlash some quarters. criticisms now seem set follow book into anglophone world, where appears next week print English first time.
When Christophe Blain began work World Without End, already France' most celebrated comic book artist recipient international awards. enviable position being able choose any creative project.
chose call Jean-Marc Jancovici, one France' foremost climate science communicators. "I frightened," said Blain, interview Guardian. "I realised climate change reality. When I'm frightened I move - I can't stay still, I jump action. action call Jean-Marc tell him let' make book together."
opportunity Jancovici, already author eight books climate breakdown energy transition, whose online lectures topics had viewed millions times, had waiting.
"I felt very excited because way I knew would work sure get audience doesn't read books who my ecosystem," said. " way reach people remained out reach before, because you can add zero number copies graphic novel compared classical essay."
Together, Blain Jancovici devised revealing deconstruction human-made processes pushed planet brink climate collapse, full incredible observations, such fact effects fossil fuel energy mean if each human had, average, 200 enslaved people working them, or, without machines, 1.5 trillion people would work produce same amount energy.
There painful truths, including little acknowledged fact 35% world' electricity still produced coal - dirtiest all fossil fuels.
book' most powerful element lies use series evocative images decode concepts energy production consumption, burden our planet.
Most notably, fossil fuel economy, all advantages given human civilisation, depicted Iron Man (or rather Armour Man UK version, reasons copyright), exoskeleton donned humanity expand powers near omnipotence.
image superhero came naturally, Jancovici said. " our superpowers come all machinery world, mixing human shape idea machines, well I either choose Terminator or Iron Man, I chose Iron Man. It' little bit friendly."
Iron Man problematic counterpart, spectre - quite literally World Without End - greenhouse gas pollution. "One metaphors used book history Faust," Jancovici said. "First you enjoy, then you pay. It' exactly what fossil fuels bringing us."
What sets World Without End apart other examinations climate breakdown look deep connection between energy abundance scientific social progress enabled - comforts cannot easily abandoned - breakdown our planet' climate.
" new part book, my view, one," said Jancovici. "It' put just one piece something gathers both knowledge physical flows our productive system our way living, major externality, climate change."
where most terrifying aspect arises. World Without End describes situation where cataclysmic effects climate change beginning grip human civilisation just people can no longer use, indeed begin run out, energy sources needed cope them.
very name book can seen précis paradox, Jancovici said. " Christophe' idea, I thought expressed very well romantic idea never-ending story growth abundance, exactly what fossil fuels brought us while. course understatement title appears anyone: world without end."
where solutions come - well book' most-contested claim. Jancovici Blain downplay potential renewable energies such wind, solar hydropower. Nuclear power, they say, only way decarbonise power grids fast while maintaining benefits industrial society.
position led criticism, even France, already gets most electricity nuclear. Renewable energy advocates accused book "pro-nuclear bias", pointing out Jancovici' connections energy industry through his think tank Shift Project.
Some campaigners even took actions into bookshops, masquerading representatives French publisher, Dargaud, persuade staff insert anti-nuclear "erratum" copies.
Jancovici said if book rewritten there would less material nuclear power, because regrets - thought position had vindicated energy crisis provoked Russia' invasion Ukraine. "Part debate France," said. "Anti-nuclear positions all mainstream media they five years ago. owe Putin, because general movement all across Europe.
" renewable energies what wanted say they totally useless, or without interest. It' they nature do properties dense dispatchable fossil fuels,, course, can do something them. won't do them what believe can do, sustain industrial civilisation renewable energies alone."
Blain Jancovici attributed book' phenomenal sales shareability, offering easy discreet way people who cared climate issue use explain problems others. "When book released, what I expected book would become viral. became viral," Blain said.
Jancovici added: "What happened book designed given, strategy became effective."
authors' hope World Without End precipitate change consciousness around energy consumption. " book made understand problem, understand orders magnitude," Blain said.
" understand what costs, really, what means, what behind scenes. When you understand, it' impossible think same way before... You can see things around you differently. You imagine your future life differently, you, or your children, your parents, anybody. You know world different."
Or, Jancovici put : "It' kilowatt hours, stupid."

Saudi Arabia driving huge global investment plan create demand oil gas developing countries, undercover investigation revealed. Critics said plan designed get countries "hooked harmful products".
Little known oil demand sustainability programme (ODSP) investigation obtained detailed information plans drive use fossil fuel-powered cars, buses planes Africa elsewhere, rich countries increasingly switch clean energy.
ODSP plans accelerate development supersonic air travel, notes uses three times jet fuel conventional planes, partner carmaker mass produce cheap combustion engine vehicle. Further plans promote power ships, use polluting heavy fuel oil or gas provide electricity coastal communities.
ODSP overseen Saudi Arabia' de facto ruler, crown prince Mohammed bin Salman, involves biggest organisations, such $700bn Public Investment Fund, world' largest oil company, Aramco, petrochemicals firm Sabic, government' most important ministries.
publicly available information, programme largely presented "removing barriers" energy transport poorer countries "increasing sustainability", example providing gas cooking stoves replace wood burning.
However, all planned projects revealed investigation Centre Climate Reporting Channel 4 News involve increasing use oil gas. official said "one main objectives".
head World Bank said recently rich countries companies needed help developing countries leapfrog fossil-fuelled economic growth past roll out renewable energy. Investors lodged 58% vote against Woodside' climate report, representing strongest protest recorded against any dozens listed companies around world regularly put climate-related resolutions shareholders.
Woodside chair, Richard Goyder, who survived push against his own re-election AGM, said disappointed result, non-binding.
" board seriously consider outcome when reviewing our approach climate change," Goyder told shareholders Perth. " take shareholder feedback seriously."
well-known businessman, who chairs Qantas AFL, defiant, telling investors Woodside' operations part solution climate change.
" world' going heck lot better off if moves coal-fired power gas-fired power soon can," said.

battle Woodside' climate plan pitted country' biggest oil gas producer against global Australian investors increasingly concerned energy sector' contribution global warming.
Critics believe Woodside' strategy overly reliant offsets, aligned Paris climate agreements, does seriously consider emissions produced those using gas.
producer criticised pursuing plans develop new fields, representing expansion fossil fuel production time opponents say sector must rein emissions.
criticism rubs against position laid out Woodside representatives, who repeatedly said company poised "thrive through energy transition" required energy security.
Woodside chief executive, Meg O'Neill, said company proud role gas playing support decarbonisation. She said some rivals had retreated climate goals after being unrealistic.
" Woodside, determined play our role addressing climate change, won't make promises can't deliver," she said Wednesday.
Climate reports, detail how company plans align operations rising environmental concerns, subject non-binding votes therefore don't automatically trigger policy change.
They, however, way shareholders express disapproval, placing pressure directors change direction, especially given some world' biggest investment pension funds, including AustralianSuper, now voted against Woodside' plans.

" inconceivable Richard Goyder' board continue trend dismissing shareholder concerns following overwhelming rejection Woodside' climate plan," Kater said.

Fossil fuel companies' funding universities' climate-focused efforts delaying green transition, according most extensive peer-reviewed study date industry' influence academia.
study, published journal WIREs Climate Change Thursday, six researchers pored thousands academic articles industries' funding research past two decades. Just handful them focused oil gas companies, showing "worrying lack attention" issue, analysis says.
even small body research shows pattern industry influence: " academic integrity higher education risk," they write.
During past two decades, non-profits, campus organizers small group scholars sounded alarm oil companies' influence academia, drawing parallels tobacco, pharmaceuticals food producers who funded scholarship.
new study, researchers found out roughly 14,000 peer-reviewed articles conflicts interest, bias research funding across all industries 2003 2023, only seven mentioned fossil fuels. When authors broadened search look book chapters, they found only seven.
even combing through small body existing scholarship, authors identified hundreds instances US, UK, Canada Australia where oil gas interests had poured funding into climate energy research while sitting advisory or governing boards, endowing academic posts, sponsoring scholarships, advising curricula or otherwise influencing universities.
" find universities established yet under-researched vehicle climate obstruction fossil fuel industry," authors write.
analysis found oil companies long influenced universities focus climate efforts would enshrine future fossil fuels, despite experts' repeated warnings world must stop burning coal, oil gas avert worst climate impacts.
" science telling us fossil fuel phase-out No 1 thing need focus, within our universities, there' very little research how do fossil fuel phase-out," said Jennie Stephens, climate justice professor Maynooth University Ireland study co-author. " provides some explanation why society so ineffective inadequate our responses climate crisis."
Fossil fuel companies' relationships universities can create potential bias research real or perceived conflicts interest, authors write.
"Our intention protect scientific integrity," said Geoffrey Supran, University Miami associate professor who studies fossil fuel industry messaging co-authored study. " want warn scholars university leaders they can pawns propaganda scheme."
BP, instance, funneled between $2.1m $2.6m Princeton University' Carbon Mitigation Initiative between 2012 2017. initiative produced research ways decarbonize economy.
"It' noteworthy scenarios decarbonization initiative outlined, only one them didn't include serious role played fossil fuels paired negative emissions technologies," said Supran.
study highlights internal 2017 campaign-strategy memo presented public relations firm BP proposed targeting Princeton "partner" help authenticate "BP' commitment low carbon" despite commitment expanding planet-heating fossil fuel production.
another example, influential 2011 study MIT Energy Initiative called gas " bridge low carbon future" even though planet-heating fossil fuel. Several study' authors had financial ties, funding, major oil gas companies.
" report helped situate natural gas, or fossil gas, part climate solution," said Stephens. " seemed reinforce Obama administration' all---above strategy," she added, referring former president' commitments supporting both fossil fuels renewables.
spokesperson MIT Energy Initiative said funders " no control" institute' reports: "no approval or rejection, no opportunity accept or reject any findings". added study question "developed vigorously debated multidisciplinary team".
earlier example, study notes 1997, Exxon paid Harvard Law School professor write "why punitive damages awards inappropriate today' civil justice system" company appealing $5bn punitive damages award following major oil tanker spill Alaska.
Reached comment, spokesperson US fossil fuel lobby group American Petroleum Institute said: "America' oil natural gas industry continue work experts organizations committed advancing solutions tackle climate change, meet growing demand ensure continued access affordable, reliable American energy." Guardian contacted BP, Exxon, Princeton Harvard; none immediately available comment.
There some evidence funding oil gas companies associated positive view fossil fuels, study notes. relationships polluting energy companies can affect internal campus decision-making, authors argue.
Universities dependent fossil fuel funding, instance, may less likely divest endowments sector, said Supran.
Despite authors' efforts, scope fossil fuel funding campus remains unclear because vast majority university research centers do disclose donors publicly. One 2023 report non-profit Data Progress found fossil fuel companies donated least $700m 27 US universities past decade, authors noted almost certainly undercount.
Universities sometimes pushed back calls transparency. Years ago, one new study' co-authors, Emily Eaton, requested her university Canada disclose fossil fuel funders; when refused do so, she took court, 2021 judge ruled her favor.
report comes amid increasing public scrutiny oil sector' relationship universities, including April report US Democrats Capitol Hill. Efforts push academic institutions "dissociate" fossil fuel companies ramping campuses across country.
" literature review confirms what students our movement known years," said Jake Lowe, executive director Campus Climate Network, pressuring schools sever ties industry. "Big oil infiltrated academia order gain undue credibility obstruct climate action."
avert these conflicts future, Stephens said governments should provide public funding universities.
" public funding help them act public good," she said.

plan create clean electricity system 2030 promised Labour before election "immensely challenging" still "credible" if ministers take urgent action fix Britain' sluggish planning system, energy system operator said.

newly formed National Energy System Operator (Neso) put forward conclusions part official advice new ministers how reach Labour election pledge decarbonise power system 2030.
Fintan Slye, chief executive Neso, said: "There' no doubt challenges ahead journey delivering clean power great. However, if scale those challenges matched bold, sustained actions outlined report, benefits delivered even greater."
Labour faced questions whether 2030 target can realistically met. Neso report said Britain' regional power networks would need grow pace four times faster past decade, while UK' high-voltage transmission network would need built twice rate previous 10 years next five.
Neso said there would need "urgent action" industry, regulators government officials reduce time took consent big projects overhaul planning process so all changes delivered "simultaneously, full maximum pace". Keir Starmer pledged "throw everything" getting UK net zero making sweeping changes planning system.
Slye said: " clean power system Great Britain deliver backbone homegrown energy breaks link between volatile international gas prices; secure affordably powers our homes buildings; decarbonises transport take school work; drives businesses today catalyses innovations future."
Neso said there two pathways towards government' goal would lead Britain generating low-carbon electricity consumes each.
first route relies heavily surge new renewable energy projects, including plans triple UK' offshore wind capacity 50 gigawatts next six years.
second path calls lower levels offshore wind favour greater reliance nuclear power extending life existing reactors building new ones within six years, alongside gas plants fitted carbon capture technology.
both pathways UK need double onshore wind capacity 13GW 2023 27GW 2030, triple solar power 15GW 47GW 2030. ambition require market flexible power demand - typically asking consumers shift use times high demand - 2GW last between 10GW 12GW 2030.
Under both scenarios, UK would continue use gas-fired power stations generate electricity when renewable or stored electricity unavailable - would make less 5% country' power consumption.
Ed Miliband, energy secretary, said: " independent report provides conclusive proof government' clean energy superpower mission right choice country, replacing Britain' dependency volatile fossil fuel markets clean, homegrown power controlled Britain.
" government determined ensure significant reforms planning grid need so can back builders support investors make once generation upgrade Britain' energy infrastructure happen."

Excess heat produced across Europe almost power entire region preventing waste largely being ignored solution energy crisis, say environmental experts.
" global energy crisis wakeup call stop wasting energy," said Toby Morgan, senior manager built environment Climate Group, environmental --profit. "Now, ever, need make better use energy already produce, simply can't afford let literally escape out window. Energy efficiency improvements, like capturing recycling excess heat, absolutely critical lower fossil fuel demand lower bills."
report published week global engineering company Danfoss estimated EU alone, excess heat equal 2,860 TWh, almost same EU' total energy demand heat hot water.
Surplus heat released into air wide range sources, including supermarkets, transport networks, data centres commercial buildings. Much can captured used via existing heat recovery technologies, such heat pumps, plus efficient air conditioners manufacturing machinery according authors report. Other solutions include improved urban planning district energy systems based networks renewable energy supplies both heating cooling.
Brian Vad Mathiesen, professor energy planning renewable energy systems Aalborg University, led research cited report builds his team' previous Heat Roadmap Europe projects. " amount cities, regions countries Europe waste heat while spending billions natural gas or electric heating mind-blowing," said Vad Mathiesen, warning Europe' energy security stake.
"Take Netherlands - there virtually no district heating even though there almost twice amount waste heat compared heat demand. Denmark same size towards 60% percent district heating only one third population. use waste heat certainly connected technical differences. While physical laws same, political traditions very different."
There "huge, unharnessed potential" excess heat produced heavy industry, such chemical manufacturing, steel cement production. EU, amounts 267 TWh, combined heat generation Germany, Poland Sweden 2021.
Data centres major consumers electricity. 2020, European data centres consumed 3.5% region' electricity demand, according ReUseHeat, EU project explored barriers urban heat recovery. report highlights UK abundance excess energy, including 456 data centres. "That' third most world, just behind Germany US," explains Kim Fausing, president chief executive Danfoss, who believes recycling heat crucial step towards green transition.
"If businesses harness all excess heat these centres, emissions savings revenue selling heat would highly significant. Greater London, identified least 648 eligible excess heat sources, including data centres, underground stations, supermarkets, wastewater treatment plants, food production facilities. Why aren't businesses local government organisations using these?" said Fausing, adding London' excess heat equated 9.5 TWh, roughly amount heat required heat 790,000 households. "Reusing excess heat offers incredible opportunities businesses throughout UK reduce emissions, save money make money. What waiting?"
rapidly tackle energy crisis, Vad Mathiesen proposes heat planning directive enables local authorities plan according local conditions. would involve mapping existing waste heat sources greater detail, then proposing thermal networks distribute heat effectively initiatives improve energy efficiency buildings.
"Energy efficiency needs top priority any business or government, particularly during energy crisis," said Morgan, who advises every corporate net zero commitment include time-bound energy efficiency target, governments incentivise uptake energy efficient technologies. "Energy efficiency improvements climate critical. time action now."

amount electricity generated UK' gas coal power plants fell 20% last, consumption fossil fuels lowest level since 1957.
since Harold Macmillan UK prime minister Beatles' John Lennon Paul McCartney met first time UK used less coal gas.
UK' gas power plants last generated 31% UK' electricity, or 98 terawatt hours (TWh), according report industry journal Carbon Brief, while UK' last remaining coal plant produced enough electricity meet just 1% UK' power demand or 4TWh.
Fossil fuels squeezed out electricity system surge renewable energy generation combined higher electricity imports France Norway long-term trend falling demand.
Higher power imports last driven increase nuclear power France hydropower Norway 2023. marked reversal 2022 when string nuclear outages France helped make UK net exporter electricity first time.
Carbon Brief found gas coal power plants made just third UK' electricity supplies 2023, while renewable energy provided single largest source power grid record 42%.
third decade renewable energy sources, including wind, solar, hydro biomass power, outperformed fossil fuels, according analysis. Renewables Britain' nuclear reactors, generated 13% electricity supplies last, helped low-carbon electricity make 55% UK' electricity 2023.
Dan McGrail, chief executive RenewableUK, said data shows " central role wind, solar other clean power sources consistently playing Britain' energy transition".
" working closely government accelerate pace build new projects new supply chains face intense global competition, everyone trying replicate our success," McGrail said.
Electricity fossil fuels two-thirds lower 2023 compared peak 2008, according Carbon Brief. found coal dropped 97% gas 43% last 15 years.
Coal power expected fall further 2024 after planned shutdown Britain' last remaining coal plant September. Ratcliffe Soar coal plant, owned German utility Uniper, scheduled shut before next winter after generating power 55 years.
Renewable energy increased sixfold since 2008 UK constructed wind solar farms, large Drax coal plant converted some generating units burn biomass pellets.
Electricity demand tumbled 22% since peak 2005, according data, part long-term trend driven energy efficient homes appliances well decline UK' manufacturing sector.
Demand electricity expected double UK aims cut emissions net zero 2050 because plan relies heavily replacing fossil fuel transport heating electric alternatives.

terminology climate ecological crises phrase "tipping point" loaded dreadful implications.
evokes climate breakdown supercharged mass escape methane locked Siberian permafrost, or great currents oceans smothered freshwater melting Greenland ice sheet, or Amazon turning great rainforest parched savannah after felling one too many trees.
come mean something very different.
Policymakers tip world into series "positive tipping points", accelerating switch fossil fuels clean energy sources, simply introducing mandates requiring key sectors make change, report claims.
researchers say such "regulatory mandates specific timeframes" stipulate certain industries make rising share sales clean technologies or phase out most polluting fuels.
Once reached, these tipping points trigger benevolent cascades related sectors, each accelerating others down path low-carbon transition, while simultaneously slashing prices consumers.
idea urgency behind. Experts say global CO2 emissions must halved 2030 if world any hope limiting global temperature rise 1.5C, beyond devastating impacts expected. Meanwhile emissions still rising.
Prof Tim Lenton Global Systems Institute (GSI) University Exeter, behind research, said: " world off course meet Paris agreement climate goals, triggering positive tipping points now only credible way limit global warming well below 2C (3.7F) above preindustrial levels.
" need rapid transformation our economies society dramatically cuts prices carbon emissions. Focusing positive tipping points benefits consumers, taxpayers, businesses people around world facing worst impacts climate change."
What latest research shows, all possible government interventions, mandates most effective way push industries towards such tipping points.
Using data 70 countries, including all big carbon emitters, GSI' researchers used modelling predict impact different kinds government interventions - taxation, subsidies regulatory mandates - encouraging decarbonisation energy generation, heating, light heavy road transport.
modelling simulated how investors or consumers choose between technologies based availability, cost historical preferences.
study found taxation - hitherto most commonly used policy - weakest intervention, while regulatory mandates had biggest impact, quickly leading adoption clean technologies levels triggered positive tipping points related industries.
Four such mandates assessed recommended report, including: phasing out coal power 2035 developed countries 2045 developing countries; requiring rising proportion heating appliance sales heat pumps 2025, reaching 100% 2035; requiring rising proportion car sales zero-emission vehicles, reaching 100% 2035; requiring rising proportion truck sales zero-emission vehicles, reaching 100% 2040.
" found policies advance low-carbon transition any one these sectors help bring forward tipping point other three," said Simon Sharpe, co-author study director -Curve Economics, nonprofit research organisation.
" example, increased use clean electricity or energy storage one sector drives innovation brings down cost these technologies, enabling faster transitions other sectors. addition, increasing electrification heating transport provides new options balancing power system, reducing cost clean power."
paper identifies "super-leverage points" greatest potential trigger cascades positive change. Dr Femke Nijsse, lead author study, said: " zero-emission mandate cars shows best potential 'super-leverage point' global transition.
" policy only highest effect within own sector, significant positive influence transition other sectors. Coal power phase-out super-leverage point, bringing forward positive tipping points heating heavy road transport sectors four years some countries."
UK preparing close last coal-fired power station, Ratcliffe--Soar Nottinghamshire, end month, make first G7 country phase out coal power.
Lenton said: "Mandates clearly most effective policy approach - call policymakers worldwide implement them speed. Failure do so high human economic costs."

report attributed China' remarkable progress expanding non-fossil energy sources range policies government implemented, including generous subsidies incentivise developers well regulations put pressure provincial governments generating companies.
China began operating world' largest hybrid solar-hydro power plant Tibetan plateau Sunday. Named Kela, plant can produce 2bn kW hours electricity annually, equal energy consumption 700,000 households.
only first phase massive clean energy project Yalong River basin. installation 20GW capacity now expected reach 50GW 2030.

way China' grid organised can incentivise building coal plants around renewable generators. Much new renewable capacity connected local energy supply often bundled coal power transmitted areas higher demand.

third Africa' great apes threatened boom mining projects minerals required renewable energy transition, new research shows.
estimated 180,000 gorillas, bonobos chimpanzees risk due increase demand critical minerals such copper, lithium, nickel cobalt, study found. Many those minerals required clean energy technologies such wind turbines electric cars. Researchers say boom demand driving destruction tropical rainforests critical habitats Africa' great apes.
"Africa experiencing unprecedented mining boom threatening wildlife populations whole ecosystems," researchers wrote paper, published Science Advances. Africa home estimated 30% world' mineral resources, substantial production increases renewable energy expected drive demand.
Mining harms apes through habitat loss, pollution disease. can make habitats accessible hunters farmers, roads carved into forest. two-thirds primate species already threatened extinction.
" shift away fossil fuels good climate must done way does jeopardise biodiversity," said lead researcher Dr Jessica Junker non-profit conservation organisation Re:wild. " current iteration may even going against very environmental goals aiming... crucial everyone adopt mindset reduced consumption."
Bwenge gorillas Bwindi Impenetrable Forest national park border Democratic Republic Congo Uganda. Photograph: Courtesy Martha M Robbins/Max Planck Institute
Understanding net impact mining energy transition minerals biodiversity challenging. climate crisis threatens great apes, clean energy technologies important avoiding worst effects global heating.
paper - written collaboration researchers German Centre Integrative Biodiversity Research Martin Luther University Halle-Wittenberg - used data operational pre-operational mining sites 17 African countries mapped areas where mining high ape densities overlapped. defined buffer area 10km around mine area would directly affected 50km buffer indirect impacts.
paper found largest mining impacts apes west African countries Liberia, Sierra Leone, Mali Guinea. Guinea, 23,000 chimpanzees (83% population) directly or indirectly affected mining activities.
Even most ecologically sensitive areas generally protected. Unrelated apes, paper found 20% mining areas overlapped regions considered unique biodiversity, or labelled "critical habitats". Junker said: "Companies, lenders nations need recognise may sometimes greater value leave some regions untouched mitigate climate change help prevent future epidemics."
Researchers said done mitigate mining' effects endangered species. Mining companies required make biodiversity data publicly available. possible impact mining projects great apes other species even higher paper found, according researchers.
Biodiversity offset schemes typically developed last long mining project does, although impacts great apes permanent. "Mining companies need focus avoiding impacts great apes much possible use offsetting last resort, there currently no example great ape offset successful," said Dr Genevieve Campbell International Union Conservation Nature, who senior researcher Re:wild.

Key points come out deal included:
* reinforced 1.5C goal recognised would require 43% emissions cut 2030 60% 2035 relative 2019 levels. implies major increase targets policies when countries submit new commitments 2025.
* Countries backed call global renewable energy tripled rate energy efficiency improvements doubled 2030.
* statement global emissions should peak 2025 dropped. China, among others, objected despite evidence may track peak own emissions then.
* Language backed fossil fuel interests found way into text, including "transitional fuels" - seen code natural gas - "carbon capture utilisation storage".
* Little progress made climate adaptation finance, deal acknowledges need trillions dollars support.
* loss damage fund help most vulnerable repair damage climate breakdown operationalised - major step forward - significant work remains build capacity.
strained nature agreement reflects UN climate conference' consensus process. Once deal reached, individual countries responsible delivering agreements through national policies investments.

large infrastructure bill passed US Senate hailed Joe Biden key tool tackle climate crisis includes billions dollars support supposedly clean fuel potentially even polluting coal, new research found.

new study found surprisingly large emissions production so-called "blue" hydrogen, variant being enthusiastically pushed fossil fuel industry probably falling under definition clean hydrogen Senate bill.
Blue hydrogen involves splitting gas into hydrogen carbon dioxide then capturing storing CO2 ensure doesn't heat planet. process involves incidental release methane, potent greenhouse gas, uses huge amount energy separate then store carbon dioxide, some escapes anyway.
means production hydrogen actually creates 20% greenhouse gases coal, commonly regarded most polluting fossil fuel, when being burned heat, 60% burning diesel, according new paper, published Energy Science & Engineering journal.
"It' pretty striking, I surprised results," said Robert Howarth, scientist Cornell University who authored paper alongside Mark Jacobson, Stanford University researcher. "Blue hydrogen nice marketing term oil gas industry keen push it' far carbon free. I don't think should spending our funds way, these sort false solutions."
Hydrogen Council, group includes oil companies BP, Total Shell among members, said hydrogen "key role play global energy transition" replacing polluting fuels, predicting account 18% total energy demand 2050.
Dozens gas companies US started producing hydrogen or testing viability existing gas pipelines, some climate campaigners said step towards entrenching fossil fuel infrastructure time when world, outlined Monday' Intergovernmental Panel Climate Change (IPCC) report, needs rapidly move net-zero emissions.
others upbeat use hydrogen help lower emissions some stubbornly polluting sectors. "Hydrogen panacea or silver bullet, appears critical decarbonization 'hard--electrify' sectors such long-haul heavy trucking, international marine shipping some parts heavy industry," said Mike Fowler, director advanced energy technology research Clean Air Task Force.
There form "green" hydrogen involves producing hydrogen water using only renewable energy, option isn't explicitly chosen funding infrastructure bill, still needs pass Democrat-held House Representatives.
" look bill see massive giveaways fossil fuel infrastructure incompatible serious climate action," said Carroll Muffett, chief executive Center International Environmental Law. "Congress went out way specify green hydrogen so funding just helps prop fossil fuel industry. potential these technologies being routinely overstated even impacts being understated."
latest IPCC report, warned "irreversible" impacts if emissions aren't drastically cut, identified methane, produced oil gas drilling animal agriculture, providing huge contribution current global heating.
